documents.title: Russian Oligarch's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions

preview_img.title: Russian Oligarch's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions

properties.title: Russian Oligarch's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions
properties.summary: The 142-meter yacht 'Nord,' owned by Russian billionaire Alexey Mordashov, successfully traversed the Strait of Hormuz on April 25, despite significant maritime traffic restrictions imposed since the start of the Middle East conflict on Feb. 28. The vessel departed Dubai for Oman, according to tracking data. Iran has severely limited navigation in the crucial waterway, which usually handles nearly 20% of global hydrocarbon exports, while granting occasional passage authorizations, particularly to Russian ships. This transit highlights exceptions made for certain vessels amidst heightened regional tensions and disrupted maritime traffic.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Step by Step: What Do We Know?

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Step by Step: What Do We Know?

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Step by Step: What Do We Know?
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: eight nautical miles west of Iran
properties.summary: On Sunday, a Navy destroyer disabled and seized the Touska, an Iranian cargo ship, in the Gulf of Oman after it tried to evade the blockade. Maritime intelligence experts say that more ships in and around the Strait of Hormuz seem to be adopting “spoofing” tactics to avoid detection. It is difficult to pass between the open ocean and a waterway as narrow as the Strait of Hormuz without being detected. The Strait of Hormuz is a strategic waterway connecting the Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Indian Ocean. Iran’s military can threaten shipping traffic throughout the Strait of Hormuz, even though much of its navy has been destroyed by US and Israeli strikes.

documents.title: Hong Kong-flagged tanker transits Strait of Hormuz amid U.S. restrictions

preview_img.title: Hong Kong-flagged tanker transits Strait of Hormuz amid U.S. restrictions

properties.title: Hong Kong-flagged tanker transits Strait of Hormuz amid U.S. restrictions
properties.summary: A Hong Kong-flagged tanker, the 'AVA 6', has successfully passed through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil transport. The vessel departed from the UAE and sailed through the gulf of Oman. While U.S. forces have stated they are not blocking the strait, they are preventing ships from entering or leaving Iranian ports. This transit occurs despite heightened tensions and a partial blockade, with traffic yet to return to pre-war levels.

documents.title: Iran to Charge Ships Billions for Passage Through Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran to Charge Ships Billions for Passage Through Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran to Charge Ships Billions for Passage Through Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Following a simulated conflict and subsequent "ceasefire" on March 15, 2026, Iran, with the implicit backing of Oman, has begun charging vessels passage fees through the Strait of Hormuz. This unprecedented move, violating international maritime law, could generate over $64 billion annually. The decision follows an alleged joint US-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran and subsequent Iranian closure of the vital waterway, which trapped nearly 2,000 ships. Negotiations mediated by Pakistan and China led to a fragile truce, with Iran asserting its right to levy fees for transit, ostensibly for rebuilding and transit control, a claim disputed by nations like the UAE and Qatar.

documents.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz, First Since Iran War Began

preview_img.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz, First Since Iran War Began

properties.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz, First Since Iran War Began
properties.summary: A French-flagged container ship, the CMA CGM Kribi, has successfully passed through the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first known transit by a Western-affiliated vessel since the Iran war disrupted the crucial waterway. The ship sailed from Dubai toward the Gulf of Oman on Thursday, signaling its French ownership while flying a Maltese flag. Traffic through the strait has been severely impacted since the conflict began over a month ago, with only a few ships affiliated with Iran-friendly nations able to pass. European countries, including France, have initiated diplomatic efforts to ease the crisis, but progress has been limited.

documents.title: Tanker Hit by Unknown Projectile in Gulf of Oman, Fire Erupts

preview_img.title: Tanker Hit by Unknown Projectile in Gulf of Oman, Fire Erupts

properties.title: Tanker Hit by Unknown Projectile in Gulf of Oman, Fire Erupts
properties.summary: A tanker navigating the Gulf of Oman was struck by an unknown projectile near the Strait of Hormuz, causing a fire on board. The incident occurred approximately 11 nautical miles east of the United Arab Emirates coast. This event follows Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global energy route, and escalates tensions in the region. A second vessel was also reportedly hit. The UK Maritime Trade Operations center confirmed the attacks but reported no casualties.

documents.title: Deteriorating Security Conditions in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Deteriorating Security Conditions in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Deteriorating Security Conditions in Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Journalists were granted rare access to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil shipping route, where maritime traffic has largely ceased due to ongoing conflict. Hundreds of vessels await passage, while small Iranian contraband boats make regular trips to Oman. The area has seen at least twenty commercial ships attacked since March 1, with two recent explosions attributed to drone strikes overnight. The strategic waterway, usually bustling with over a hundred daily transits, is now eerily calm, with local fishing communities impacted by the halt in activity and restricted sea access.

documents.title: Iran Mines Threaten Shipping in Strait of Hormuz, Experts Warn

preview_img.title: Iran Mines Threaten Shipping in Strait of Hormuz, Experts Warn

properties.title: Iran Mines Threaten Shipping in Strait of Hormuz, Experts Warn
properties.summary: Reports suggest Iran has laid naval mines in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global energy supplies. These underwater explosives are designed to detonate on contact or proximity, posing a significant threat to maritime traffic. The strait, which connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman, is vital for transporting approximately 20% of the world's traded oil. Tanker companies have already suspended operations through the area due to heightened tensions.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Tensions Rise Amid Maritime Incidents, GPS Jamming

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Tensions Rise Amid Maritime Incidents, GPS Jamming

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Tensions Rise Amid Maritime Incidents, GPS Jamming
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about “5 nautical miles north of Musandam
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz has become a new flashpoint following U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, leading to maritime incidents and electronic warfare activities. International maritime authorities have reported several attacks on commercial vessels, including explosions and fires near the UAE and Oman. Extensive GPS and AIS interference has affected over 1,000 ships in the region, with some vessels appearing erroneously located on land. Shipping companies like Maersk are rerouting services due to heightened security risks, and experts warn the Persian Gulf is currently the most dangerous area for commercial shipping.

documents.title: Hormuz Strait Traffic Drops 70% After Iran Attack; Global Oil Routes Threatened

preview_img.title: Hormuz Strait Traffic Drops 70% After Iran Attack; Global Oil Routes Threatened

properties.title: Hormuz Strait Traffic Drops 70% After Iran Attack; Global Oil Routes Threatened
properties.summary: Commercial shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has significantly declined by 70% following a joint U.S. and Israeli attack on Iran, according to ship-tracking data. Most vessels in the area have either turned back, rerouted, or are stalled in the Gulf of Oman, impacting major oil and LNG exporters like Saudi Arabia, Iraq, and the UAE. While the Iranian military warned ships to avoid the strait, citing safety concerns, a U.S. official stated there is no evidence Iran is attempting a military blockade. The disruption threatens global oil supply and Iran's own oil exports, amid warnings from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy about closing the waterway.

properties.location_metadata__relative_area: 16 nautical miles north of Oman
properties.summary: A maritime security incident occurred Tuesday off the Strait of Hormuz when armed boats attempted to intercept a commercial vessel 16 nautical miles north of Oman. The boats, whose origin remains unknown, hailed the ship and demanded it stop, but the vessel continued on its planned course without direct contact. Relevant authorities have launched an investigation into the incident. This event occurs amid heightened tensions in the Persian Gulf region, a critical route for global oil supplies.

properties.summary: Iran has reportedly begun mass suppression of GPS signals and other navigation systems in the Strait of Hormuz, leading to the accumulation of ships in the Gulf of Oman. Saudi Arabia has raised its armed forces to maximum readiness, while Bahrain and Qatar have declared a state of emergency.

documents.title: Breaking: Maritime Incident Occurs Near Strait of Hormuz Amidst Iran-Israel Conflict

preview_img.title: Breaking: Maritime Incident Occurs Near Strait of Hormuz Amidst Iran-Israel Conflict

properties.title: Breaking: Maritime Incident Occurs Near Strait of Hormuz Amidst Iran-Israel Conflict
properties.summary: A maritime incident occurred near the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil transit route, on Tuesday, June 17, 2025. A British maritime security firm reported the incident, noting it took place 22 nautical miles east of Khorfakkan, UAE. Fires were reported on three ships in the Gulf of Oman before entering the strait. Simultaneously, Iran continued missile attacks on Israel, with dozens of rockets launched early Tuesday, marking the third attack in 12 hours.

documents.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Oil Supply After Israeli Airstrikes

preview_img.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Oil Supply After Israeli Airstrikes

properties.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Oil Supply After Israeli Airstrikes
properties.summary: Iran is considering closing the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global trade route, in response to Israeli airstrikes. The narrow strait, controlled by Iran, Oman, and the UAE, sees 20% of the world's oil traffic and one-third of its liquefied natural gas. Such a move could escalate tensions, potentially leading to military conflict with Israel and the United States, which could disrupt maritime traffic.

documents.title: Gas tanker crosses Strait of Hormuz amid Iran war concerns

preview_img.title: Gas tanker crosses Strait of Hormuz amid Iran war concerns

properties.title: Gas tanker crosses Strait of Hormuz amid Iran war concerns
properties.summary: A liquefied natural gas tanker operated by ADNOC has traversed the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such passage since the Iran war began. Shipping data indicates the vessel is nearing the Indian coast, potentially signaling an improvement in Gulf shipping traffic. Previous attempts by Qatari tankers to cross in April were unsuccessful, though an empty Omani tanker did pass earlier that month. Experts suggest this single passage is an early positive sign but not a guarantee of normal traffic due to the volatile security situation.

preview_img.title: Cruise Ship First to Transit Strait of Hormuz Since War Began

properties.title: Cruise Ship First to Transit Strait of Hormuz Since War Began
properties.summary: Iran and the United States announced that the strategically important Strait of Hormuz will be fully open to commercial vessels throughout the Lebanon ceasefire. A Malta-flagged cruise ship, reportedly traveling without passengers and bound for Oman, departed Dubai on Friday after a 47-day layover, according to MarineTraffic. The Celestyal Discovery is expected to arrive in Oman on Saturday. The announcement by Tehran and Washington is considered critical for restoring trade in the region, which was severely affected by the hostilities.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Largely Closed Despite Ceasefire; Ship-tracking data shows only 4 ships passed

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Largely Closed Despite Ceasefire; Ship-tracking data shows only 4 ships passed

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Largely Closed Despite Ceasefire; Ship-tracking data shows only 4 ships passed
properties.summary: DUBAI, Apr 9: The Strait of Hormuz remained largely shut to commercial shipping on Wednesday, the first day of a declared ceasefire between the United States and Iran, according to ship-tracking data and industry reports. Data from maritime analytics firm Kpler showed that only four vessels with active Automatic Identification System (AIS) trackers passed through the strategic waterway during the day. These vessels are widely believed to transport sanctioned Iranian crude oil to international markets, outside standard monitoring systems. Separately, The New York Times reported that the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed for tankers nearly 24 hours after the ceasefire announcement, with no oil or liquefied gas carriers observed transiting the waterway. Shipping companies and insurers are also said to be exercising caution, contributing to the slowdown as risk assessments remain elevated despite the ceasefire announcement.

documents.title: French Container Ship Safely Navigates Strait of Hormuz as Traffic Resumes

preview_img.title: French Container Ship Safely Navigates Strait of Hormuz as Traffic Resumes

properties.title: French Container Ship Safely Navigates Strait of Hormuz as Traffic Resumes
properties.summary: A container ship operated by French shipping firm CMA CGM has become the first major Western-operated vessel to safely transit the Strait of Hormuz since the conflict began. Three Omani oil tankers also passed through the vital waterway. The CMA CGM Kribi maintained its Automatic Identification System (AIS) transponder throughout the journey, a move contrasted with earlier 'dark voyages' where ships turned off tracking to avoid potential attacks. While traffic has slightly increased, it remains below normal levels, with Iran reportedly establishing protocols for passage, favoring allied nations.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Oil Trade

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Oil Trade

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Oil Trade
properties.summary: Since the start of the Iran war on February 28, the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global oil trade, has been effectively blocked. Iran has severely restricted ship traffic through the strait, causing significant disruption. According to the UK Maritime Trade Operations, 23 commercial vessels, including ten tankers, have reported attacks or security incidents in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, or Gulf of Oman since the beginning of March. The conflict has resulted in the deaths of at least eight seafarers or port workers, with four missing and ten injured.

properties.summary: A third cargo ship was attacked by an "unknown projectile" near the Strait of Hormuz on March 11, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations. The incident follows two earlier attacks that day, one causing a fire off Oman and another damaging a vessel near the UAE. These attacks bring the total number of targeted ships to 13 since the start of the conflict. The Strait of Hormuz has become a focal point for maritime escalation, with Iran targeting global shipping.

documents.title: Trump promises protection for ships in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Trump promises protection for ships in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Trump promises protection for ships in Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: US President Donald Trump says he intends to secure shipping traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, which is threatened by Iran, including with the US Navy. “If necessary, the United States Navy will begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, as soon as possible,” he posted on his platform Truth Social on Tuesday. The strait between Iran and Oman is considered one of the most important shipping routes worldwide. Significant oil-producing countries are located along the Gulf, including Iran, Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, and the United Arab Emirates. The war and threats from Iran to set passing ships on fire have caused unrest in the energy markets.

documents.title: Trump Threatens Iran Navy; Hormuz Shipping Plunges 70%

preview_img.title: Trump Threatens Iran Navy; Hormuz Shipping Plunges 70%

properties.title: Trump Threatens Iran Navy; Hormuz Shipping Plunges 70%
properties.summary: Shipping traffic through the vital Strait of Hormuz has plummeted by 70% following attacks by the U.S. and Israel against Iran, with most vessels rerouting or waiting in the Gulf of Oman. This disruption to a key global energy route raises concerns about international oil and gas supplies, with Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, and Qatar being the most affected. Iranian state media reported warnings from the military that the waterway is unsafe, though a U.S. official stated there's no evidence of a military blockade. Analysts suggest a full blockade is difficult for Iran, but harassment and selective attacks remain possible, while President Trump vowed to "utterly destroy" the Iranian navy.

documents.title: Iranian boats approach US-flagged tanker in Strait of Hormuz, maritime sources say

preview_img.title: Iranian boats approach US-flagged tanker in Strait of Hormuz, maritime sources say

properties.title: Iranian boats approach US-flagged tanker in Strait of Hormuz, maritime sources say
properties.summary: A map showing the Strait of Hormuz and Iran is seen in this illustration taken June 22, 2025. REUTERS/Dado Ruvic/IllustrationDUBAI, Feb 3 (Reuters) - A group of Iranian gunboats approached a U.S.-flagged tanker in the Strait of Hormuz north of ​Oman, maritime sources and a security consultancy said on Tuesday. The vessel did not enter Iranian internal territorial waters and was escorted by a U.S. warship, the maritime risk ⁠management group said. The agency said it was investigating the ​incident, which happened in the inbound Traffic Separation Scheme of the ‌Strait of Hormuz. OPEC members Saudi Arabia, Iran, ​the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq export most of their crude via the strait, mainly to Asia.

documents.title: Iran Shura Council recommends closure of Hormuz Strait after US strike

preview_img.title: Iran Shura Council recommends closure of Hormuz Strait after US strike

properties.title: Iran Shura Council recommends closure of Hormuz Strait after US strike
properties.summary: Iran's Guardian Council recommends closing the key Strait of Hormuz after US strikes on major Iranian nuclear facilities, raising fears of global energy supply disruptions. Navy via AP)Iran’s Shura Council has recommended the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in response to the recent US military strikes targeting the country’s nuclear facilities. The Strait of Hormuz, situated between Oman and Iran, links the Gulf to the north with the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea to the south. Qatar, a top global exporter of liquefied natural gas (LNG), ships nearly all of its LNG through the Strait of Hormuz. More recently, in May 2019, four oil vessels, including two Saudi tankers, were attacked off the UAE coast just outside the Strait.

properties.summary: Two tankers collided near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday amidst GPS navigation disruptions linked to military actions between Israel and Iran. The conflict, which began last Friday, is not seen as an immediate threat to shipping in the critical corridor. However, increased caution is thinning the ranks of incoming ships and raising their costs. The Strait of Hormuz, separating Iran and Oman and connecting the Persian Gulf to the Indian Ocean, is a vital artery for global trade, carrying about a quarter of the world's crude oil and a third of its liquefied natural gas.

documents.title: Russian Superyacht Nord Navigates Strait of Hormuz Amid Iranian Blockade

preview_img.title: Russian Superyacht Nord Navigates Strait of Hormuz Amid Iranian Blockade

properties.title: Russian Superyacht Nord Navigates Strait of Hormuz Amid Iranian Blockade
properties.summary: A 142-meter Russian superyacht named Nord, valued at $500 million, has successfully navigated the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial maritime chokepoint currently considered a war theater between Iran and the U.S. The vessel, owned by sanctioned Russian oligarch Alexei Mordașov, traveled from Dubai to Muscat, Oman, over the weekend. This passage occurred despite Iran's recent actions to close and reopen the strait, adding a layer of complexity to geopolitical tensions in the Persian Gulf.

documents.title: Iran Seizes Two Ships in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran Seizes Two Ships in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran Seizes Two Ships in Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: eight nautical miles west of Iran
properties.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard seized two cargo ships attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz, according to state media. The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) reported an attack by an Iranian gunboat on a commercial vessel in the strait, northeast of Oman. The IRGC reportedly fired on one vessel, causing significant damage to its bridge, though the crew was unharmed. Iran claims the ships lacked necessary permits for passage and are being directed toward its shores.

documents.title: First Civilian Ship Crosses Strait of Hormuz Amid Mideast Conflict

preview_img.title: First Civilian Ship Crosses Strait of Hormuz Amid Mideast Conflict

properties.title: First Civilian Ship Crosses Strait of Hormuz Amid Mideast Conflict
properties.summary: The first civilian vessel, the Celestyal Discovery cruise ship, has traversed the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the Middle East conflict. The Maltese-flagged ship, carrying no passengers, departed Dubai on Friday and is en route to Oman. This reopening of the maritime route follows announcements from Iran and the United States that the strait would be fully open to commercial traffic during a fragile truce. Despite the de-escalation in maritime activity, the regional situation remains volatile, with Hezbollah and Iran warning of potential retaliation against any violations of the ceasefire by Israel.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Sees Increased Ship Traffic Amid Iran Blockade

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Sees Increased Ship Traffic Amid Iran Blockade

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Sees Increased Ship Traffic Amid Iran Blockade
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint partially blocked by Iran due to war, has experienced a surge in ship passages in recent days. Data indicates 16 freighters passed through on Wednesday and 11 on Tuesday, a notable increase though still below pre-war levels. Notably, an LNG tanker, the Sohar, and a container ship belonging to French giant CMA CGM were among those transiting. Iran's deputy foreign minister suggested a new mechanism coordinated with Oman to regulate traffic, implying it would ensure safe passage.

documents.title: Iran Allows Limited Ship Passage Through Strait of Hormuz to Assert Control

preview_img.title: Iran Allows Limited Ship Passage Through Strait of Hormuz to Assert Control

properties.title: Iran Allows Limited Ship Passage Through Strait of Hormuz to Assert Control
properties.summary: Iran is allowing passage for a small number of ships through the Strait of Hormuz, a strategic waterway for global oil trade, to demonstrate its dominance. Recent reports indicate a drastic 95% decrease in vessel traffic compared to pre-war levels, with only about 8 to 99 ships passing through in recent weeks. Some vessels are reportedly turning off their Automatic Identification System (AIS) or taking longer routes, potentially in agreement with Iranian authorities. This limited passage is seen as a strategic move by Iran to project power and potentially ease diplomatic isolation, especially with key trading partners like China.

documents.title: The first non-Iranian tanker to cross the Strait of Hormuz with an activated tracking device

preview_img.title: The first non-Iranian tanker to cross the Strait of Hormuz with an activated tracking device

properties.title: The first non-Iranian tanker to cross the Strait of Hormuz with an activated tracking device
properties.summary: London (AFP) - A non-Iranian oil tanker has crossed the Strait of Hormuz and is broadcasting signals via a tracking device for the first time since the outbreak of war in the Middle East on February 28, according to what the Marine Traffic Observatory, which specializes in monitoring maritime navigation, announced Monday. The Observatory said in a statement, “The Afrimax oil tanker (meaning a medium-sized tanker) Karachi, loaded with DAS crude oil from Abu Dhabi, has become the first non-Iranian tanker to cross the strait, and it is broadcasting an Automatic Identification System (AIS) signal, which indicates that some shipments may receive safe, negotiated transit.” He pointed out that the ship, which is 237 meters long and flies the flag of Pakistan, has a draft (the part submerged in water) of 11.5 metres, which leads to suspicion that it is heavy and loaded with cargo. The Observatory noted that “this crossing comes after weeks of severely reduced maritime navigation at the strategic water crossing.” The Strait of Hormuz, through which one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas production passes, is a strategic corridor for oil and gas exports from the Gulf states.

The impact ignited a fire onboard, forcing the crew to evacuate. The incident occurred approximately 20 kilometers north of the Omani coast. This follows another reported attack on a container ship off the coast of the UAE earlier in the week. Tensions remain high in the region since the start of the Iran-Ukraine war.

documents.title: GPS jamming hits the Strait of Hormuz.

preview_img.title: GPS jamming hits the Strait of Hormuz.

properties.title: GPS jamming hits the Strait of Hormuz.
properties.summary: GPS jamming hits the Strait of Hormuz. GPS jamming hits the Strait of Hormuz. WIRED reports that GPS jamming attacks have disrupted more than 1,100 ships in the Strait of Hormuz since the first US-Israeli strikes against Iran on February 28th. GPS interference was common in the region before the outbreak of war, but Windward is now tracking "at least 21 new AIS jamming clusters across the UAE, Qatari, Omani, and Iranian waters." The researchers note, "Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz slowed, with some Western-affiliated tankers transiting dark or reversing course."

documents.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Trade

preview_img.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Trade

properties.title: Iran Considers Closing Strait of Hormuz, Threatening Global Trade
properties.summary: The Iranian parliament has reportedly approved closing the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial shipping route, but the decision requires final approval. The move could endanger global trade by shutting the narrow passage between Iran and Oman. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical point in global energy trade, with the equivalent of 21% of global oil consumption passing through it daily. British maritime trade operations are assessing threats to commercial shipping near Iran as "high."

documents.title: Why is the Strait of Hormuz Important for Global Oil Supply?

preview_img.title: Why is the Strait of Hormuz Important for Global Oil Supply?

properties.title: Why is the Strait of Hormuz Important for Global Oil Supply?
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: 22 nautical miles east of Khor Fakkan
properties.summary: Tensions between Iran and Israel have raised concerns about disruptions to oil and ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. The Strait of Hormuz, located between Oman and Iran, is a vital waterway connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. Approximately 20 million barrels of crude oil, condensate, and fuel pass through this waterway daily. China, India, Japan, and South Korea are the most vulnerable if the supply is disrupted.

documents.title: Egyptian Maritime Official: Straits of Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb Closed During Iranian Attack on Israel

preview_img.title: Egyptian Maritime Official: Straits of Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb Closed During Iranian Attack on Israel

properties.title: Egyptian Maritime Official: Straits of Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb Closed During Iranian Attack on Israel
properties.summary: According to the head of the Egyptian Maritime Authority, Iran closed the Straits of Hormuz and enlisted the Houthis in Yemen to close Bab el-Mandeb at the start of its military strike on Israel. The closures were intended to prevent ships from passing through during the operations due to potential joint attacks from U.S. bases in Qatar, the UAE, Oman, and Kuwait. After confirming that the U.S. did not participate in any strikes, Iran reopened shipping traffic in the straits, seeking to avoid escalating the conflict in the region.

documents.title: Oligarch's Superyacht Allowed Through Strait of Hormuz by US, Iran

preview_img.title: Oligarch's Superyacht Allowed Through Strait of Hormuz by US, Iran

properties.title: Oligarch's Superyacht Allowed Through Strait of Hormuz by US, Iran
properties.summary: A superyacht linked to sanctioned Russian oligarch Alexei Mordashov, valued at over $500 million, passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday. The vessel, named "Nord," departed from Dubai and arrived in Muscat, Oman, on Sunday, according to maritime tracking data. The circumstances of its passage remain unclear, as Iran has severely restricted traffic through the vital waterway since late February, and the United States has imposed a blockade on Iranian ports. Russia and Iran have strengthened cooperation in recent years, with the Iranian foreign minister visiting Moscow just days prior.

documents.title: Iran Fires on Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Causing Heavy Damage

preview_img.title: Iran Fires on Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Causing Heavy Damage

properties.title: Iran Fires on Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Causing Heavy Damage
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: eight nautical miles west of Iran
properties.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps fired on a commercial vessel in the Strait of Hormuz, causing significant damage to the ship's bridge, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO). The incident occurred 15 nautical miles northeast of Oman when an Iranian vessel approached the container ship and opened fire without prior contact. All crew members are reported to be safe. Iranian state media described the action as an "enforcement action" and warned that the strait would remain under strict control until U.S. restrictions on shipping to Iran are lifted.

documents.title: First cruise ship passes through Strait of Hormuz after 2-month blockade

preview_img.title: First cruise ship passes through Strait of Hormuz after 2-month blockade

properties.title: First cruise ship passes through Strait of Hormuz after 2-month blockade
properties.summary: A Malta-flagged cruise ship has passed through the Strait of Hormuz after departing Dubai, where it had been held for 47 days. The vessel, the "Celestial Discoverer," is the first passenger ship to transit the strategic waterway since the start of the conflict. It is expected to arrive in Oman tomorrow, sailing without passengers. The United States and Iran announced earlier today that the Strait would be fully open to commercial traffic by the end of the ceasefire.

documents.title: Iran and Oman to charge ships passing through Strait of Hormuz during ceasefire, state media says

preview_img.title: Iran and Oman to charge ships passing through Strait of Hormuz during ceasefire, state media says

properties.title: Iran and Oman to charge ships passing through Strait of Hormuz during ceasefire, state media says
properties.summary: Iran and Oman plan to charge transit fees for vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz during the two-week ceasefire, according to Iran’s semi-official Tasnim News Agency. The funds will be earmarked for reconstruction, Tasnim reported. The strait has been effectively closed since the war began, with maritime tracking data showing that only about 5% of the pre-war volume of shipping is getting through. Some tankers have been able to pass through; for instance, Pakistan and India have negotiated with Iran for guaranteed passage of some of their flagged vessels. Iran has reportedly been charging up to $2 million per vessel for passage trough Hormuz.

documents.title: Bahrain Proposes UN Resolution to Reopen Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Bahrain Proposes UN Resolution to Reopen Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Bahrain Proposes UN Resolution to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Bahrain has circulated a draft UN Security Council resolution proposing the use of force to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping lane. The proposal, reportedly influenced by France, aims to counter Iran's potential disruption of maritime traffic. However, Russia and China have expressed reservations, favoring diplomatic solutions over military action. Iran, meanwhile, is working with Oman on an alternative protocol to ensure safe passage, viewing control of the strait as more vital than its nuclear program.

documents.title: Iran Effectively Blocks Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Oil Flow

preview_img.title: Iran Effectively Blocks Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Oil Flow

properties.title: Iran Effectively Blocks Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Oil Flow
properties.summary: Iran has effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial global oil transit route, following attacks on the country by the U.S. and Israel. Approximately 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas typically passes through this narrow corridor, and the conflict's escalation has led to a sharp increase in global fuel prices. The strait, situated between Iran and Oman, connects the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea, with an estimated 20 million barrels of oil and petroleum products passing daily. The disruption has caused shipping traffic to plummet, with numerous vessels reporting attacks, resulting in casualties and a significant rise in oil prices.

properties.title: Tanker Karaczi Breaks Through Strait of Hormuz, Transmitting AIS Signal
properties.summary: The tanker Karaczi, carrying crude oil from Abu Dhabi, entered the Iran-controlled waters of the Strait of Hormuz on Sunday. It is the first tanker from a country other than Iran to pass through the strait and transmit an Automatic Identification System (AIS) signal since the start of the war. The activation of the AIS suggests an agreement has been reached for safe passage. Shipping traffic in the strait had largely ceased following US and Iranian actions, impacting global oil and gas exports and fertilizer supplies. The US has announced plans to escort tankers through the strait.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Becomes Hotspot as Four Ships Attacked in Hours

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Becomes Hotspot as Four Ships Attacked in Hours

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Becomes Hotspot as Four Ships Attacked in Hours
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai
properties.summary: At least four vessels, including ships from Greece, Japan, Thailand, and Israel, were attacked near the Strait of Hormuz within a few hours on Wednesday. The attacks occurred in a key global shipping route for oil and other commodities. The UK Maritime Trade Operations reported projectiles hitting ships northwest of Dubai and north of Oman, causing fires and damage. Iran's Revolutionary Guard claimed responsibility for some attacks, threatening to block oil exports.

documents.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Closure Amid Tensions, Alternative Proposed

preview_img.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Closure Amid Tensions, Alternative Proposed

properties.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Closure Amid Tensions, Alternative Proposed
properties.summary: Iran has repeatedly threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway for global oil transport, citing U.S. sanctions and Israeli attacks. These threats, which have occurred multiple times since 2018, raise concerns about global economic stability and oil prices. To counter potential disruptions, an alternative sea channel connecting the UAE to the Gulf of Oman is proposed. This project, involving the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and potentially the U.S., aims to bypass the strait and ensure continued East-West trade.

documents.title: German Giant Halts Shipping Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

preview_img.title: German Giant Halts Shipping Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

properties.title: German Giant Halts Shipping Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions
properties.summary: German shipping company Hapag-Lloyd has suspended all traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital global waterway, due to rising tensions and frequent attacks in the Middle East. The decision follows an attack on an oil tanker near Oman on Sunday, which injured four people. While official channels have not announced a closure, maritime authorities are warning of increased threats to shipping. At least 150 tankers are reportedly waiting outside the Strait, which connects the Persian and Oman Gulfs and is crucial for the region's oil exports.

properties.title: Reports: Fire on 3 Ships in Sea of Oman Before Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Reports indicate a maritime incident occurred near the Strait of Hormuz. A British company, Ambrey, reported an incident 22 nautical miles east of Khorfakkan in the Emirates. Media outlets circulated images purportedly showing a fire on three ships or oil tankers in the Sea of Oman, before approaching the Strait of Hormuz. The incident occurred about 40 kilometers from Khorfakkan, near the Strait of Hormuz at the entrance to the Gulf.

documents.title: Ships Advised to Avoid Red Sea, Report Hormuz Passages After Israeli Strikes on Iran

preview_img.title: Ships Advised to Avoid Red Sea, Report Hormuz Passages After Israeli Strikes on Iran

properties.title: Ships Advised to Avoid Red Sea, Report Hormuz Passages After Israeli Strikes on Iran
properties.summary: Following Israeli strikes against Iran, merchant shipping continues through the Strait of Hormuz, but some shipowners are seeking to avoid the region. Greece and the UK have recommended their merchant fleets avoid sailing in the Gulf of Aden and register all passages through the Strait of Hormuz. The Combined Maritime Force, led by the United States, stated that the Strait of Hormuz remains open and commercial traffic continues without interruption. However, they added that events have significantly increased the risk of regional conflict.

documents.title: Russian Oligarch's Superyacht Safely Navigates Tense Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Russian Oligarch's Superyacht Safely Navigates Tense Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Russian Oligarch's Superyacht Safely Navigates Tense Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: A 142-meter luxury superyacht, the North, owned by Russian steel magnate Alexey Mordashov, has successfully passed through the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial and currently tense maritime route. The vessel, valued at over 570 million euros, traveled from Dubai to Muscat, Oman, with its journey completed on Sunday morning. The yacht's passage raises questions about how it obtained permission through the strait, which Iran has been restricting, potentially involving a 'transit fee'. The United States has been intercepting vessels with ties to Iran in the region.

documents.title: Iran's Strait of Hormuz route stalls as shippers opt for pre-war path

preview_img.title: Iran's Strait of Hormuz route stalls as shippers opt for pre-war path

properties.title: Iran's Strait of Hormuz route stalls as shippers opt for pre-war path
properties.summary: Maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz has experienced chaos and paralysis in recent days following Iran's announcement of a full reopening, which was followed by a renewed closure. Amidst new blockades by the US in the Gulf of Oman and Iran's attack on two Indian vessels, shipping companies, transporters, and insurers face extreme uncertainty. Hundreds of ships are trapped, with a clear consensus that the current situation is untenable for navigation. The alternative route proposed by Iran lacks minimal safety guarantees, and a return to the pre-war situation is deemed necessary for the secure transport of vital raw materials from Persian Gulf countries.

documents.title: Iranian Gunboats Fire on Oil Tanker in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iranian Gunboats Fire on Oil Tanker in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iranian Gunboats Fire on Oil Tanker in Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Two Iranian gunboats opened fire on an oil tanker attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz northeast of Oman on April 18, 2026. The UK Maritime Trade Operations, based in Dubai, reported the incident. Earlier, Iranian naval forces reportedly forced two Indian ships, including a supertanker carrying 2 million barrels of oil from Iraq, to retreat from the strait amid exchanges of fire. This event follows Iran's announcement to close the Strait of Hormuz again in retaliation for U.S. sanctions on its ports.

properties.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Citing Mines; Warns U.S.
properties.summary: Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil shipping lane, citing the presence of mines and warning that passage is not guaranteed until the U.S. ends its "aggression" in the region. Maritime tracking showed a tanker turning back near Oman, and Iranian state media suggested naval forces mined the strait. This closure, which impacts global oil trade, comes amidst ongoing tensions with the U.S. and following Israeli attacks on Lebanon. Iran insists on safe passage according to international norms once U.S. aggression ceases.

documents.title: Iran Imposes Maritime Toll in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran Imposes Maritime Toll in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran Imposes Maritime Toll in Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Iran is now imposing a maritime toll in the Strait of Hormuz, with approximately ten vessels managing to pass through daily. This morning, three boats reportedly used an alternative route along Oman's southern coast, providing proof of Omani nationality. Oman and Iran are reportedly working on a navigation protocol for the area post-conflict. Meanwhile, Iran's Revolutionary Guard has permitted 20 Pakistani ships to use their corridor, while maintaining strict filtering for non-allied countries.

documents.title: First Non-Iranian Oil Tanker Crosses Strait of Hormuz Since War Began

preview_img.title: First Non-Iranian Oil Tanker Crosses Strait of Hormuz Since War Began

properties.title: First Non-Iranian Oil Tanker Crosses Strait of Hormuz Since War Began
properties.summary: The oil tanker 'Karachi', carrying crude oil from Abu Dhabi, became the first non-Iranian vessel to transmit an Automatic Identification System (AIS) signal while crossing the Strait of Hormuz since the Middle East war began on February 28. The ship, flying a Pakistani flag, is reported to be heavily laden and is now sailing in the Gulf of Oman. This transit occurs after weeks of significantly reduced maritime traffic in the strategic waterway, which handles one-fifth of the world's oil and LNG production and has been largely closed due to threats from Iran.

Concerns over potential mines have already impacted shipping traffic, with few vessels seen navigating the waters off Oman's coast. The attacks come amid a broader conflict where the UAE has reported over 1,475 Iranian drone and 270 missile strikes, causing civilian casualties from falling debris.

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Crucial to Global Oil, Gas; Iran-Israel Tensions Threaten Supply
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, separating Iran and Oman, is a vital chokepoint for global energy transit, carrying approximately 20% of the world's crude oil and significant natural gas. Tensions between Iran and Israel, particularly following recent attacks, raise concerns about potential supply disruptions and the closure of the strait. Around 144 vessels, including a substantial portion of oil tankers, pass through daily. Iran has previously threatened to block maritime traffic in response to sanctions and military actions.

documents.title: Armed vessels attempt to intercept ship in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Armed vessels attempt to intercept ship in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Armed vessels attempt to intercept ship in Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: 16 nautical miles north of Oman
properties.summary: Several armed vessels attempted to intercept a ship approximately 16 nautical miles north of Oman in the Strait of Hormuz, the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) reported Tuesday. The group of small, armed boats tried to make radio contact with the vessel, which ignored the demands and continued on its planned route. The UKMTO is investigating the incident, which occurred within the traffic separation system of the Strait of Hormuz. This strait is a critical chokepoint for global oil exports, and Iran has previously seized vessels in the vicinity.

documents.title: Iran’s Parliament approves decision to close strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran’s Parliament approves decision to close strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran’s Parliament approves decision to close strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: 22 June 2025 18:55 (UTC+04:00)The Iranian parliament has approved a decision regarding the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Azernews reports, citinf foreign media. It should be noted that the Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. At its narrowest point, the strait is about 21 miles wide, with two shipping lanes two miles wide in each direction. During the Iran-Iraq War in the 1980s, Iran targeted oil tankers and oil loading facilities with mines and missiles, including Chinese-made Silkworm cruise missiles, and used speedboats to harass tankers. These actions did not fully block the strait but caused sharp increases in shipping insurance premiums and delayed maritime traffic.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Collapse Risks Alert World Economy Amid Israel-Iran Tensions

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Collapse Risks Alert World Economy Amid Israel-Iran Tensions

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Collapse Risks Alert World Economy Amid Israel-Iran Tensions
properties.summary: Tensions between Israel and Iran have raised concerns about the potential collapse of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global oil supply. A recent collision of two oil tankers off the coast of Oman has added to these concerns. While neither country has directly targeted strategic facilities, the conflict has led to a modest decline in merchandise traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and increased freight rates for tankers traveling from the Persian Gulf to China. The Strait of Hormuz is a vital waterway for the transport of about 20% of the world's oil consumption.

documents.title: Two cargo ships attacked near Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Two cargo ships attacked near Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Two cargo ships attacked near Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: eight nautical miles west of Iran
properties.summary: Two cargo ships were attacked in recent hours near the Strait of Hormuz. In the first incident, an armed vessel belonging to Iran's Revolutionary Guard fired upon a container ship 15 nautical miles northeast of Oman, causing significant damage to the bridge. The crew is safe and there were no environmental repercussions. The second attack involved a cargo ship being fired upon eight nautical miles northwest of Iran, with the crew also reporting to be safe and no damage to the vessel. These incidents occur shortly after the U.S. president extended a ceasefire with Iran.

documents.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage for Ships Through Strait of Hormuz via Oman Side

preview_img.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage for Ships Through Strait of Hormuz via Oman Side

properties.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage for Ships Through Strait of Hormuz via Oman Side
properties.summary: Iran has proposed a new plan for ship traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting allowing vessels safe passage through Oman's waters. This proposal was made during talks with the United States and is contingent on an agreement to prevent new conflicts. Under the plan, Iran would not interfere with ships in Omani waters while maintaining control over its own territorial sea. This move is seen as a de-escalation from previous aggressive stances, such as threatening fees for passage, which were met with international rejection. The International Maritime Organization welcomed any steps towards ensuring safe transit through the established traffic separation scheme in this vital waterway, through which about 20% of the world's oil and LNG supply passes.

documents.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz Since Cease-Fire

preview_img.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz Since Cease-Fire

properties.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz Since Cease-Fire
properties.summary: The first non-Iranian oil tanker has traversed the Strait of Hormuz since a fragile cease-fire was announced between Iran and the United States. The Gabon-flagged tanker, the MSG, passed through the strategic waterway on Thursday carrying approximately 7,000 tons of Emirati fuel oil bound for India. While the strait is crucial for global energy transport, maritime traffic has not significantly resumed since the two-week truce began Wednesday. The incident suggests a potential easing of tensions in the critical shipping lane.

While the strait is crucial for global energy transport, maritime traffic has not significantly resumed since the two-week truce began Wednesday. The incident suggests a potential easing of tensions in the critical shipping lane.

documents.title: Some Shipping Traffic Resuming Through Strait Of Hormuz

properties.title: Some Shipping Traffic Resuming Through Strait Of Hormuz
properties.summary: New posts14:36Some Shipping Traffic Resuming Through Strait Of HormuzReuters and Iran's Tasnim news agency, which is close to the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps, reported on April 4 that Iran has allowed vessels carrying essential goods through the Strait of Hormuz, including humanitarian aid and basic supplies, to reach its ports, provided they coordinate with local authorities. Since April 3, ships from Oman, Pakistan, Turkey, India, France, and Japan have passed through the strait, according to MarineTraffic and LSEG. Lines, became the first Japan-linked vessel and liquefied natural gas (LNG) carrier to transit through the strait since the conflict began. Based on vessel-tracking data from MarineTraffic and LSEG, overall traffic is estimated at around 512 percent of prewar levels. The UN Security Council plans to vote next week on a Bahraini resolution to protect shipping.

documents.title: Three vessels hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Three vessels hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Three vessels hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Three vessels have been hit by unknown projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, maritime security agencies and sources said on Wednesday, as one of the strikes led to a fire onboard a ship and forced most of its crew to evacuate it. The Thailand-flagged bulk carrier Mayuree Naree was targeted and damaged approximately 11 nautical miles north of Oman, two maritime security sources said. A third vessel, a bulk carrier, was also hit by an unknown projectile approximately 50 miles northwest of Dubai, maritime security firms said. The projectile had damaged the hull of the Marshall Islands-flagged Star Gwyneth, maritime risk management company Vanguard said, adding that the vessel’s crew were safe. Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery accounting ​for about 20% of global oil and gas supply, has dropped rapidly since the Iran conflict began on February 28.

properties.title: Iran Halts Commercial Shipping in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions
properties.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps has effectively blocked commercial ship traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, a vital chokepoint for global energy trade. The action follows retaliatory strikes between Iran and Israel, with Iran targeting Israeli and U.S. interests. Although Iran's foreign minister denied intentions to fully close the strait, a tanker was struck and began sinking off the coast of Oman. Global maritime traffic has decreased by 40-70%, leading to a 10% rise in oil prices and increased insurance premiums.

documents.title: Oil Prices Surge as Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Energy Supply

preview_img.title: Oil Prices Surge as Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Energy Supply

properties.title: Oil Prices Surge as Strait of Hormuz Blockade Disrupts Global Energy Supply
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime artery between Iran and Oman, is experiencing a total paralysis, causing unprecedented disruption to global economic stability. Several Greek fleets have rerouted, and Japanese oil tankers are returning, while shipments from Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE are stalled. This blockade halts a fifth of global seaborne oil and all of Qatar's LNG exports, leading to an immediate 12% surge in oil prices. The economic repercussions are expected to extend to gas stations, airlines, industries, and international supply chains worldwide, with significant implications for countries like Mexico.

properties.title: Cruise Ship First to Traverse Strait of Hormuz After Iran Reopens Waterway
properties.summary: The Celestyal Discovery, a cruise ship carrying approximately 1,360 passengers, successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz on Friday after Iran reopened the vital waterway. This marks the first commercial vessel to pass through since Iran threatened to attack ships, following the U.S. and Israel's war against Tehran. The ship departed from Port Rashid in Dubai and is heading to Muscat, Oman. Iran's foreign minister announced the strait was fully open to all commercial vessels during a temporary 10-day truce, though later indicated it would close again if U.S. maritime blockades persist.

documents.title: Few Vessels Cross Strait of Hormuz as Shipping Traffic Remains Severely Restricted

preview_img.title: Few Vessels Cross Strait of Hormuz as Shipping Traffic Remains Severely Restricted

properties.title: Few Vessels Cross Strait of Hormuz as Shipping Traffic Remains Severely Restricted
properties.summary: DUBAI, Apr 9: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains heavily disrupted despite a ceasefire between the United States and Iran, with only a limited number of vessels managing to pass through the strategic waterway in the past 24 hours, according to ship-tracking data cited by Reuters and maritime intelligence firms. Data from Lloyd’s List Intelligence showed that just one oil products tanker and five dry bulk carriers transited the strait, while overall maritime movement continues to hover near a standstill. On the first day of the ceasefire, only three vessels reportedly passed through, all of which had current or previous links to Iran. Most permitted vessels are believed to have connections to Iran, though some Indian ships have reportedly passed through following diplomatic intervention by New Delhi. He said freedom of navigation in the strait must be guaranteed without conditions, adding that any attempt to control passage for political leverage amounts to coercion and threatens global energy security.

Traffic through the Strait has been severely restricted, with only a few ships associated with countries friendly to Tehran able to pass under an Iranian-approved system.

documents.title: French Journalist Notes Eerie Calm in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

preview_img.title: French Journalist Notes Eerie Calm in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

properties.title: French Journalist Notes Eerie Calm in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions
properties.summary: A French journalist described a paradoxical calm in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global trade route, despite heightened international tensions. Nicolas Bertrand of France Télévisions observed an Omani frigate on alert and a damaged oil tanker attacked by Iran, alongside Iranian smugglers using high-speed boats. The usual daily traffic of over 100 commercial vessels has drastically reduced, leaving the strait eerily quiet. This situation unfolds amid global concerns from Washington to Beijing regarding the strategic waterway.

documents.title: Maritime and port workers: how is the Middle East conflict affecting you?

preview_img.title: Maritime and port workers: how is the Middle East conflict affecting you?

properties.title: Maritime and port workers: how is the Middle East conflict affecting you?
properties.summary: The conflict in the Middle East is disrupting shipping across the region, including in the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s busiest maritime routes. Maritime traffic through the strait, the narrow channel linking the Persian Gulf with the Gulf of Oman, has effectively been closed since strikes on Iran began. Some vessels have been diverted or delayed and ports and shipping companies are dealing with heightened security concerns and uncertainty. Meanwhile, at least six major cruise ships, each carrying thousands of tourists, are anchored in or close to harbours across the region, their passengers confined to the shipsWe would like to hear from maritime workers, port staff and shipping crews about how the situation is affecting your work.

properties.summary: A container ship and a liquefied natural gas tanker have transited the Strait of Hormuz, marking rare passages through the vital waterway which has been largely blocked since the start of the conflict in Iran. The CMA CGM Kribi, owned by a French company, sailed from the Dubai area, passing close to the Iranian coast before signaling its position near Muscat. The LNG tanker Sohar, registered as Omani, also departed the Persian Gulf via the strait, traveling along the southern route. This is the first LNG tanker passage since the conflict began, with traffic largely restricted to vessels pre-approved by Iran.

properties.summary: A Pakistani oil tanker, the Karachi, has successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such passage by a non-Iranian vessel carrying crude oil. The tanker, en route from Abu Dhabi, passed through the strategic waterway on Sunday, March 16, 2026. Shipping traffic data indicates the vessel is now proceeding into the Gulf of Oman. This event is being closely monitored within the context of regional maritime security and oil transport.

documents.title: Cargo Ship Catches Fire in Strait of Hormuz After Missile Strike

preview_img.title: Cargo Ship Catches Fire in Strait of Hormuz After Missile Strike

properties.title: Cargo Ship Catches Fire in Strait of Hormuz After Missile Strike
properties.summary: A cargo ship caught fire in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after being struck by a missile. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations, run by the British military, reported the incident occurred in the strait north of Oman. The ship's crew is evacuating. This event follows U.S. actions targeting Iranian mine-laying vessels near the entrance to the Persian Gulf. Iran has not immediately claimed responsibility for the attack.

documents.title: UN’s International Maritime Organization: Mideast war traps 20,000 seafarers, 15,000 cruise passengers in Gulf

preview_img.title: UN’s International Maritime Organization: Mideast war traps 20,000 seafarers, 15,000 cruise passengers in Gulf

properties.title: UN’s International Maritime Organization: Mideast war traps 20,000 seafarers, 15,000 cruise passengers in Gulf
properties.summary: The maritime sector said it had designated the Strait of Hormuz, Gulf of Oman and Gulf as a “warlike operations area”, granting seafarers additional protections as the Middle East war engulfs the crucial energy transit route. “I reiterate my call for all shipping companies to exercise maximum caution when operating in the affected region,” he added. Iran has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s crude and considerable supplies of liquefied natural gas travel. Several shipping groups, including Danish shipping giant Maersk, have suspended bookings in the Gulf. Energy intelligence firm Kpler said oil tanker transits through the Strait had dropped by 90 percent from last week.

documents.title: Iran Threatens Hormuz Strait Closure After Khamenei Assassination

preview_img.title: Iran Threatens Hormuz Strait Closure After Khamenei Assassination

properties.title: Iran Threatens Hormuz Strait Closure After Khamenei Assassination
properties.summary: Following the reported assassination of Iranian leader Ali Khamenei in Israeli attacks, Iran is expected to retaliate by closing the Strait of Hormuz to international shipping. This vital waterway, through which an estimated 20% of global oil supplies and 25% of liquefied natural gas pass, connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. Such a closure would have severe repercussions for the global economy and energy markets, particularly impacting Asian markets which receive a significant portion of LNG. Maritime insurance experts warn of a sharp increase in shipping costs and potential rerouting of vessels to avoid the Strait.

documents.title: Amid Tensions, Iran's Threat to Close Strait of Hormuz Raises Ownership Questions

preview_img.title: Amid Tensions, Iran's Threat to Close Strait of Hormuz Raises Ownership Questions

properties.title: Amid Tensions, Iran's Threat to Close Strait of Hormuz Raises Ownership Questions
properties.summary: Iran has threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz amid rising concerns of a potential war with Israel. While Iran legally controls the Strait at its narrowest point, international conventions state it cannot impede the passage of foreign vessels. Despite this, Iran has used the Strait as leverage in disputes, as seen in past seizures of Greek tankers in May 2022 and a container ship linked to Israel in April. The majority of the Strait is in Oman, making a full closure difficult.

documents.title: Russian Billionaire's Superyacht Nord Passes Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions

preview_img.title: Russian Billionaire's Superyacht Nord Passes Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions

properties.title: Russian Billionaire's Superyacht Nord Passes Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Restrictions
properties.summary: The superyacht Nord, owned by Russian billionaire Alexey Mordashov, has passed through the Strait of Hormuz despite maritime restrictions. The 142-meter yacht departed Dubai on the night of April 25 and proceeded towards Oman. Mordashov, Russia's wealthiest individual with an estimated $37 billion fortune, is subject to sanctions from the US, UK, and EU. This passage occurs as Iran continues to limit access through the crucial oil transit route, reportedly granting exemptions to certain Russia-linked vessels. The Strait of Hormuz remains a focal point of regional tensions following the outbreak of war in late February.

documents.title: Iran Accused of Firing on Containership in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran Accused of Firing on Containership in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran Accused of Firing on Containership in Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: eight nautical miles west of Iran
properties.summary: A containership was severely damaged after coming under fire in the Strait of Hormuz, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO). The UKMTO reported that an Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) vessel approached the containership and opened fire without warning, damaging the bridge. The incident occurred approximately 15 nautical miles northeast of Oman, and all crew members are reported to be safe. This marks another escalation of tensions in the vital shipping lane.

documents.title: Iran Fires on Tanker in Strait of Hormuz, UK Navy Says

preview_img.title: Iran Fires on Tanker in Strait of Hormuz, UK Navy Says

properties.title: Iran Fires on Tanker in Strait of Hormuz, UK Navy Says
properties.summary: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps fired on at least one ship in waters near Oman after Tehran again closed the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, according to the UK's Maritime Trade Organization. The IRGC approached an oil tanker about 32 kilometers northeast of the Gulf nation and opened fire, according to the tanker's commander. Audio recordings indicate two Indian vessels were forced out of the strait following the shots. The incident marks a dangerous turn in an already tense standoff over a critical global shipping chokepoint.

documents.title: Ships Transit Hormuz Strait Amid Tensions, Iran Eyes New Mechanism

preview_img.title: Ships Transit Hormuz Strait Amid Tensions, Iran Eyes New Mechanism

properties.title: Ships Transit Hormuz Strait Amid Tensions, Iran Eyes New Mechanism
properties.summary: Maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has increased recently, with 16 freighters passing on Wednesday compared to 11 the previous day, according to data firm Windward. While traffic is up, it remains significantly below pre-war levels, with many ships navigating close to the Iranian coast, a corridor Windward suggests allows Iran to maintain a selective blockade. Notably, the LNG tanker Sohar became the first of its kind since the conflict began to transit the strait, traveling near the Omani coast. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister suggested a new mechanism for regulating ship passage in coordination with Oman, aimed at facilitating safe transit, though specific details remain undisclosed.

documents.title: Non-Iranian Tanker Karachi Passes Strait of Hormuz for First Time Since February

preview_img.title: Non-Iranian Tanker Karachi Passes Strait of Hormuz for First Time Since February

properties.title: Non-Iranian Tanker Karachi Passes Strait of Hormuz for First Time Since February
properties.summary: The tanker Karachi, carrying crude oil from Abu Dhabi, became the first non-Iranian vessel to transit the Strait of Hormuz with its AIS signal active since the start of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Data from MarineTraffic indicates the ship, owned by Pakistan's National Shipping Corporation, entered Iran's exclusive economic zone on March 15 and passed through the strait, heading towards Pakistan. Shipping through the strait had largely halted due to escalating tensions between the U.S., Israel, and Iran. The activated AIS signal suggests the vessel received a coordinated safe passage.

documents.title: Gas loadings continue in Iran despite Hormuz halt

preview_img.title: Gas loadings continue in Iran despite Hormuz halt

properties.title: Gas loadings continue in Iran despite Hormuz halt
properties.summary: OIL and gas carriers were berthed in Iranian terminals on Monday, while outbound tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz essentially ground to a halt. However, at least half a dozen shadow fleet* gas carriers that arrived in the Gulf of Oman in the days since the war started have remained at anchor south of the strait, Lloyd’s List Intelligence data showed. Commenting on the IRGC’s commanders’ remarks, maritime risk management firm Vanguard said on Monday that there was “no verified indication of a formally enacted blockade or disruption to commercial traffic”. Despite the risks, Lloyd’s List understands that a small group of shipowners has expressed a willingness to resume transits at night and with AIS switched off, and were confident that insurance could be found. The discussions were highly speculative and did not lead to any fixtures by Monday, Lloyd’s List was told.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Crucial Amid Iran Attacks, Oil Supply Concerns

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Crucial Amid Iran Attacks, Oil Supply Concerns

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Crucial Amid Iran Attacks, Oil Supply Concerns
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a vital global strategic point separating Iran and Oman, is critical to international oil and gas transport, carrying about 20% of global crude production. Recent attacks on Iran by Israel and the U.S. have led to reports of Iran closing maritime traffic through the strait, potentially impacting the oil market due to supply disruptions. This narrow waterway, only 54 kilometers wide at its narrowest, sees an average of 144 vessels daily, including a significant number of tankers. Tensions have escalated over years, with Iran previously threatening to block the strait in response to sanctions.

documents.title: Oil Tanker Collides With Two Ships Near Strait of Hormuz; 24 Evacuated

preview_img.title: Oil Tanker Collides With Two Ships Near Strait of Hormuz; 24 Evacuated

properties.title: Oil Tanker Collides With Two Ships Near Strait of Hormuz; 24 Evacuated
properties.summary: A tanker collided with two other vessels near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, prompting the evacuation of 24 people. The incident occurred 22 nautical miles east of Khor Fakkan in the UAE and is not related to security issues, according to maritime security firm Ambrey. There has been increased electronic interference with navigation systems in the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf, impacting commercial ships. The Strait of Hormuz connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea and is a vital route for global oil transport.

documents.title: LNG tanker passes through Strait of Hormuz for first time since March

preview_img.title: LNG tanker passes through Strait of Hormuz for first time since March

properties.title: LNG tanker passes through Strait of Hormuz for first time since March
properties.summary: An liquefied natural gas (LNG) tanker has transited the Strait of Hormuz for the first time since Iran effectively closed the route in early March. The Mubaraz, operated by UAE's Adnoc, was fully loaded with 132,890 cubic meters of LNG. The vessel reportedly turned off its AIS transponder for a month before resuming transmissions near India. Approximately 20% of global LNG trade normally passes through the vital waterway.

documents.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Shipping

preview_img.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Shipping

properties.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Disrupting Global Shipping
properties.summary: Iran's military has closed the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway for global oil transport, just a day after announcing its opening. The decision by hardliners overrides the moderate government's negotiation stance, impacting nearby Oman. The port of Sinahs in Oman, a resting point for ships passing through the strait, is now deserted with dozens of vessels idled offshore. This closure has also diminished Oman's role as a neutral mediator between the U.S. and Iran, a position now largely held by Pakistan, and has led to direct attacks on Omani infrastructure, highlighting the escalating impact of the conflict on neighboring Gulf states.

documents.title: Msc Euribia Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Attacks on Indian Ships

preview_img.title: Msc Euribia Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Attacks on Indian Ships

properties.title: Msc Euribia Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Attacks on Indian Ships
properties.summary: Confusion and uncertainty surround the Strait of Hormuz as maritime traffic faces risks. The Italian cruise ship Msc Euribia successfully navigated the strait after being held up since late March. However, at least two Indian cargo ships, the Al Hasnain and Al Simnan, reported being hit by gunfire while attempting to transit the same area. Maritime security sources confirmed the attacks, though the extent of the damage remains unclear. The incidents highlight the volatile situation in the region, with vessels choosing routes near Oman to avoid Iranian-controlled waters.

documents.title: Container ship with declared French ownership passes through Hormuz Strait

preview_img.title: Container ship with declared French ownership passes through Hormuz Strait

properties.title: Container ship with declared French ownership passes through Hormuz Strait
properties.summary: A container ship declaring itself to have a French owner passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, according to Marine Traffic data analyzed by AFP on Friday. The Maltese-flagged Kribi, belonging to the French maritime transport group CMA CGM, crossed the strait -- which has been virtually blocked by Iran since early in the Middle East war -- to exit the Gulf on Thursday afternoon, the maritime tracking website showed. The ship was off Muscat, Oman, on Friday morning, still broadcasting the message "owner France" on its transponder system in the field usually corresponding to the destination.

documents.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz With Tracker Active Since War Began

preview_img.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz With Tracker Active Since War Began

properties.title: First Non-Iranian Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz With Tracker Active Since War Began
properties.summary: The Karachi, a non-Iranian oil tanker carrying crude from Abu Dhabi, became the first such vessel to transit the Strait of Hormuz while broadcasting its Automatic Identification System (AIS) signal. This development, announced by maritime tracking firm MarineTraffic, suggests that some shipments may be receiving negotiated safe passage amid the ongoing Middle East conflict which began on February 28. The European Union's foreign policy chief, Kaja Kallas, has emphasized the gravity of closing the Strait of Hormuz, a point of discussion among EU foreign ministers concerned about its impact on oil prices and supply chains.

documents.title: Missiles Strike Three Cargo Ships in Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Missiles Strike Three Cargo Ships in Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Missiles Strike Three Cargo Ships in Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai
properties.summary: Three cargo ships were struck by missiles in the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf on Tuesday. An unidentified missile hit a container ship north of Oman, causing a fire and prompting the evacuation of its crew. Another cargo vessel was damaged off the coast of the United Arab Emirates, but its crew was safe. A Thai bulk carrier was also targeted while transiting the strait, with rescue efforts underway for three remaining crew members.

documents.title: Malta-flagged container ship struck off Oman as Iran escalates Gulf tensions

preview_img.title: Malta-flagged container ship struck off Oman as Iran escalates Gulf tensions

properties.title: Malta-flagged container ship struck off Oman as Iran escalates Gulf tensions
properties.summary: Muscat: A Malta-flagged container ship was struck off the coast of Oman on Wednesday while transiting the Strait of Hormuz, two maritime security agencies have reported, as Iran stepped up its retaliatory campaign in the Gulf. Shipping tracker Marine Traffic reported the ship had been en route from Ghantoot port in the United Arab Emirates to Jeddah in Saudi Arabia. It was the fourth incident in the region within 24 hours, following strikes or near-misses affecting three other vessels off the coasts of the UAE and Oman. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said on Wednesday that they had “complete control” over the strategic waterway, through which around 20 percent of global seaborne oil passes, warning that any ships attempting to transit risked damage from missiles or stray drones. With energy prices already surging, US President Donald Trump said the US Navy was prepared to escort oil tankers through the vital shipping route.

documents.title: Iran blocks Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global trade

preview_img.title: Iran blocks Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global trade

properties.title: Iran blocks Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global trade
properties.summary: Iran has blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global trade, in response to U.S. and Israeli attacks. Multiple shipping companies, oil corporations, and trading houses have halted voyages through the strait, affecting oil, fuel, LNG, and other goods. Satellite imagery indicates at least 150 tankers are stranded, with dozens more awaiting passage. The U.S. Navy has warned against navigating the Persian Gulf and surrounding waters due to security concerns. The strait, connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman, is vital for energy transport, with approximately one-fifth of global oil consumption passing through it daily.

documents.title: First Cruiser Passes Through Hormuz Strait Since War's Start

preview_img.title: First Cruiser Passes Through Hormuz Strait Since War's Start

properties.title: First Cruiser Passes Through Hormuz Strait Since War's Start
properties.summary: The first cruiser since the start of the war has passed through the Hormuz Strait. The Maltese-flagged ship departed Dubai, where it had been anchored for 47 days, and is headed for Oman. It is reportedly sailing without passengers. This transit occurred hours after Iran and the U.S. announced the strategic waterway would fully reopen to commercial traffic by the end of a ceasefire. The vessel is expected to arrive in Oman on Tuesday.
