documents.title: Iran Allows Essential Goods Ships Through Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran Allows Essential Goods Ships Through Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran Allows Essential Goods Ships Through Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Iran has permitted ships carrying essential goods to pass through the Strait of Hormuz to its ports, according to a message reported by Tasnim News Agency on Saturday. Vessels heading to Iranian ports, including those currently in the Gulf of Oman, must coordinate with authorities and adhere to approved protocols for passage. This move follows Iran's de facto closure of the strait, a vital global shipping lane, in response to U.S. and Israeli attacks that began on February 28.

documents.title: The breakthrough could be here: a tanker has passed through the Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: The breakthrough could be here: a tanker has passed through the Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: The breakthrough could be here: a tanker has passed through the Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: The traffic of tankers in the Strait of Hormuz could start again after weeks of hardly any ships passing through the strategically important waterway. The most important sign of the resumption of traffic was the appearance of an Aframax tanker named Karachi. The Abu-Dzabi Das crude oil vessel was the first to pass through the strait with the AIS beacon on while carrying a non-Iranian cargo. According to MarineTraffic data, the 237-meter-long tanker entered Iran's exclusive economic zone at 11:33 UTC on March 15. Due to the escalation of tensions, more than twenty long-range tankers have left the Persian Gulf region.

documents.title: Projectiles Hit 3 Ships Near Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Projectiles Hit 3 Ships Near Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Projectiles Hit 3 Ships Near Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai
properties.summary: Three ships were struck by unidentified projectiles near the Strait of Hormuz in recent hours, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency. One container ship reported damage 25 nautical miles northwest of Ras Al Jaima. Later, a cargo ship caught fire and its crew evacuated after being hit 11 nautical miles north of Oman in the Strait of Hormuz. A third vessel reported an impact about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai. The incidents occurred amid heightened tensions between the U.S. and Iran.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Declared War Zone Amid Iran Conflict, Maritime Sector Says

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Declared War Zone Amid Iran Conflict, Maritime Sector Says

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Declared War Zone Amid Iran Conflict, Maritime Sector Says
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, along with the Gulf and Gulf of Oman, has been classified as a "war operating zone" by the maritime sector. This decision follows significant disruptions and risks to civilian crews due to the ongoing war in Iran. The International Transport Federation and global employers cited hundreds of ships blocked and an estimated 20,000 sailors immobilized in the region. The International Maritime Organization reported seven incidents in the Strait of Hormuz since Saturday, resulting in two deaths and six injuries.

documents.title: Shipping Traffic Increases Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran's Maritime Blockade

preview_img.title: Shipping Traffic Increases Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran's Maritime Blockade

properties.title: Shipping Traffic Increases Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran's Maritime Blockade
properties.summary: Shipping data indicates a significant rise in vessel traffic through the Strait of Hormuz following the implementation of Iran's maritime blockade. While tanker and cargo ship movements have notably increased, they remain substantially lower than pre-blockade levels. Over 20 ships passed through the strait in the 24 hours leading up to Friday, a stark contrast to the usual 150 daily transits before Iran's blockade, which had dropped to three in recent months. The U.S. initiated this blockade on Monday, April 14, aiming to prevent Iranian-linked vessels from reaching global markets, though reports suggest at least eight Iranian oil tankers have entered the Persian Gulf from the Sea of Oman.

documents.title: Europe Celebrates as First Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions

preview_img.title: Europe Celebrates as First Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions

properties.title: Europe Celebrates as First Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions
properties.summary: A container ship owned by French CMA CGM has become the first Western-linked vessel to transit the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the regional conflict. The Malta-flagged CMA CGM Kribi departed Dubai waters Thursday, sailing along Iran's coast through a designated corridor. The ship had been waiting in the Persian Gulf since early March due to the conflict's impact on trade. Iran has announced plans with Oman to ensure maritime traffic through the strait, potentially introducing transit fees and requiring data submission for approval.

This transit suggests that some vessels may have secured safe passage through negotiations, despite ongoing disruptions in the vital waterway. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global oil and LNG transportation, with approximately 20% of global supply passing through it.

properties.title: Oman Rescues 24 Sailors From Malta-Flagged Ship Hit by Missiles
properties.summary: Omani naval forces rescued 24 sailors from the Malta-flagged cargo ship 'Safeen Prestige' on Wednesday after it was struck by two missiles in the Strait of Hormuz. The ship, traveling from the UAE to Saudi Arabia, suffered a fire in its engine room above the waterline. This incident occurs amidst heightened tensions in the region, with Iran warning ships about potential missile and drone damage in the vital oil transit route. Global shipping companies are increasingly hesitant to transit the Strait due to the rising risks and associated insurance concerns.

documents.title: Iran Halts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz Amid Escalating Tensions

preview_img.title: Iran Halts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz Amid Escalating Tensions

properties.title: Iran Halts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz Amid Escalating Tensions
properties.summary: Shipping traffic has halted in the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway between Iran and Oman that carries 20% of global oil, following U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran. The Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps reportedly signaled that no vessels were permitted to pass, causing oil tankers to queue outside the strait. The incident comes as Maersk announced a suspension of its ship passages through the Bab al-Mandab strait and Suez Canal, rerouting vessels around the Cape of Good Hope. Separately, the oil tanker "Skylight" was targeted in the strait, with four crew members injured, in the first such targeting incident.

documents.title: Pakistani Tanker First to Transit Strait of Hormuz Since U.S. Blockade

preview_img.title: Pakistani Tanker First to Transit Strait of Hormuz Since U.S. Blockade

properties.title: Pakistani Tanker First to Transit Strait of Hormuz Since U.S. Blockade
properties.summary: A Pakistani oil tanker, the Shalamar, became the first vessel to transit the Strait of Hormuz with crude oil cargo since the U.S. imposed a blockade on April 13. The tanker passed south of Iran's Larak Island late Thursday, entering the Gulf of Oman with approximately 450,000 barrels of crude loaded in the UAE. The U.S. blockade requires shipowners to obtain permission from both Iranian and American authorities to transport oil from the Persian Gulf. This transit marks a slight reopening of the vital waterway after a significant slowdown in oil shipments.

documents.title: Gulf States Urge UN Force Authorization to Open Hormuz Strait

preview_img.title: Gulf States Urge UN Force Authorization to Open Hormuz Strait

properties.title: Gulf States Urge UN Force Authorization to Open Hormuz Strait
properties.summary: The Secretary General of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) has urgently appealed to the United Nations to authorize the use of force to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has allegedly blocked. The GCC, representing Saudi Arabia, UAE, Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait, and Oman, called on the UN Security Council to take necessary measures to ensure safe international navigation. This plea comes as the Security Council debates a resolution, supported by the US but met with skepticism from France, Russia, and China, which proposes authorizing states to use 'defensive' means to secure the vital shipping lane. A vote on the resolution has been indefinitely postponed.

This action, stemming from a conflict that began in late February, significantly impacts the global energy market.

documents.title: Iran Restricts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz After Attacks

preview_img.title: Iran Restricts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz After Attacks

properties.title: Iran Restricts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz After Attacks
properties.summary: Following attacks by Israel and the United States on Iran over the weekend, Iran has restricted shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global energy trade. The strait, located between Iran and Oman, is a narrow waterway connecting the Persian Gulf to the open oceans. Iranian authorities are reportedly instructing ships not to use the passage, with threats of seizure, impacting at least 25 vessels. This restriction has led to a significant increase in oil and natural gas prices globally, with Brent crude temporarily rising over 10% and natural gas up 25%. Experts warn that prolonged disruptions could severely impact world oil markets, potentially pushing Brent crude towards $100 per barrel and increasing inflation while decreasing economic growth.

documents.title: Tensions escalate as ships attacked in the Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Tensions escalate as ships attacked in the Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Tensions escalate as ships attacked in the Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Two vessels were attacked Sunday in the Strait of Hormuz, maritime security agencies said, as Iran pressed a second day of strikes in response to US-Israeli air raids. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) said one ship off the coast of Oman was struck by an unknown projectile above the waterline. Private maritime security firm Vanguard Tech said the second vessel, a tanker, was located about 17 nautical miles northwest of Mina Saqr in the United Arab Emirates. Iranian state television reported that an oil tanker struck while transiting the strait was “sinking,” broadcasting images of thick black smoke rising from the vessel, without providing further details. Danish giant Maersk announced it was suspending all vessel crossings through the strait until further notice, citing safety concerns.

documents.title: First cruise ship crosses Strait of Hormuz since Iran war began

preview_img.title: First cruise ship crosses Strait of Hormuz since Iran war began

properties.title: First cruise ship crosses Strait of Hormuz since Iran war began
properties.summary: The cruise ship Celestyal Discovery, flagged in Malta, has crossed the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such transit since the conflict with Iran began. The vessel departed Dubai after being anchored for 47 days and is en route to Oman. This passage follows announcements by Tehran and Washington that the strategic waterway, largely blocked since the conflict's start, would fully reopen for commercial traffic during a truce. The ship is reportedly traveling without passengers.

documents.title: Malta-flagged container ship first to cross Hormuz Strait since March 1

preview_img.title: Malta-flagged container ship first to cross Hormuz Strait since March 1

properties.title: Malta-flagged container ship first to cross Hormuz Strait since March 1
properties.summary: A Maltese-flagged container ship became the first to transit safely through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday since March 1, according to Marine Traffic data analysed by AFP. The vessel's navigation data showed it crossed via a new Iranian-approved route through its waters, dubbed the "Tehran Toll Booth" by leading shipping journal Lloyd's List. The few commercial vessels transiting through the waterway with their transponders on since the start of the war have passed close to Larak Island, according to maritime data analysed by AFP. Three other tankers, including one co-owned by a Japanese company, also crossed the strait by hugging close to Oman's shore, a rare transit route. Beijing expressed "gratitude" on Tuesday after three of its ships passed through the strait, including two container ships on Monday belonging to state-owned shipping giant Cosco.

properties.title: Ship on fire in Strait of Hormuz, two more struck
properties.summary: Three ships have been hit in the Strait of Hormuz and the Arabian Gulf in the space of a few hours. The UKMTO said that a cargo ship was on fire in the Strait of Hormuz after being struck by an unknown projectile 11 nm of Oman. In an update the UKMTO said fire on board the ship had been extinguished and skeleton crew remained on the vessel. A container ship was damaged by a projectile 25nm northwest of Ra’s al Khaymah, UAE, the UKMTO reported with all crew members safe and accounted. Iran has threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz and burn ships attempting to pass through the key waterway that links the Gulf region with the rest of the world.

documents.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions with US, Israel

preview_img.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions with US, Israel

properties.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions with US, Israel
properties.summary: Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, threatening to burn any ships attempting passage, following joint attacks by the United States and Israel. This vital waterway transports one-fifth of the world's total oil consumption and connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. Major oil producers like Saudi Arabia, Iran, the UAE, Kuwait, and Iraq, as well as Qatar, the world's largest LNG producer, rely on this strait for exports. Following Iran's threats, Qatar halted its gas production on Monday.

The warning from the IRGC was broadcast to ships amid retaliatory strikes by Iran against aggression from the United States and Israel. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical global oil shipping route connecting major Persian Gulf producers to the Gulf of Oman. The IRGC's move raises concerns about potential disruptions to global oil supply.

properties.title: Iran Asserts Control Over Strait of Hormuz, Sets Terms for Passage
properties.summary: Iran has demonstrated its ability to control maritime traffic in the critical Strait of Hormuz, announcing on April 17th a temporary opening for commercial vessels amid a fragile two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan. The terms stipulate only commercial ships are allowed, military presence is excluded, vessels must follow designated Iranian routes, and passage is coordinated with Iranian forces. This move highlights Iran's strategic naval power, allowing it to influence international markets and assert control over a vital global economic artery.

properties.title: Ships Resume Transit Through Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran Conflict
properties.summary: A growing number of ships are successfully transiting the Strait of Hormuz, a key global oil and gas route, despite access restrictions related to the conflict between Iran, the United States, and Israel. Maritime intelligence firm Windward reported a noticeable increase in vessel traffic, with 16 ships passing on Wednesday alone. Ships from France, Japan, and Oman were among those that navigated the strait on Thursday, utilizing approved routes. While traffic volume remains below normal levels, Iran has granted limited access to certain allied nations, suggesting a selective blockade.

documents.title: Iran War Disrupts Strait of Hormuz Oil Trade, Asia Hit Hardest

preview_img.title: Iran War Disrupts Strait of Hormuz Oil Trade, Asia Hit Hardest

properties.title: Iran War Disrupts Strait of Hormuz Oil Trade, Asia Hit Hardest
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil transport route, has seen almost no major ship traffic since a war broke out last Saturday. Following U.S. and Israeli attacks, Iran's military advised ships to avoid the strait due to insecurity. Several oil tankers have reportedly been attacked in the region, with one catching fire off the coast of Oman. This disruption significantly impacts Asia, which relies heavily on the strait for its oil and LNG imports, potentially triggering price shocks and supply chain issues worldwide.

documents.title: Elevated threat to shipping in Strait of Hormuz persists amid fresh strikes

preview_img.title: Elevated threat to shipping in Strait of Hormuz persists amid fresh strikes

properties.title: Elevated threat to shipping in Strait of Hormuz persists amid fresh strikes
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about “5 nautical miles north of Musandam
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a major shipping route between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is facing a fresh day of disruption amid ongoing strikes across the Middle East. ⁠Due to the security situation in ⁠the ‌region, German ⁠shipping group Hapag-Lloyd suspended all ⁠vessel transit through the Strait until further ⁠notice, ⁠it said on Sunday, according to Reuters news agency. It’s the only way to ship crude from the oil-rich Persian Gulf to the rest of the world. Oman’s maritime security center said Sunday that an oil tanker, called “Skylight,” was attacked about five nautical miles off the coast of Masandam in Oman, injuring four people. Later Sunday, Iran’s semi-official Mehr News Agency reported that a tanker, which was struck after attempting an “unauthorized passage through the Strait of Hormuz,” is now said to be sinking after sustaining damage.

documents.title: Iran preparing joint Hormuz navigation protocol with Oman

preview_img.title: Iran preparing joint Hormuz navigation protocol with Oman

properties.title: Iran preparing joint Hormuz navigation protocol with Oman
properties.summary: TEHRAN: Iran said on Thursday it was drafting a peacetime protocol that would supervise maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz with Oman, state media reported, with Tehran’s closure of the vital oil corridor roiling the global economy. Deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi told Russia’s Sputnik state media that the protocol would apply after the ongoing war with the United States and Israel had ended, setting basic rules to manage ship movements, the IRNA news agency said. “We are currently finalising the drafting of this protocol and, once it has been finalised internally, we will undoubtedly begin negotiations with the Omani side in order to reach a joint protocol,” he said. Oman has yet to report any such negotiations. Its foreign minister said last month his country was “working intensively to put in place safe passage arrangements for the Strait of Hormuz”.

documents.title: Iran Strait Restrictions Halt 49 Container Ships Bound for UAE

preview_img.title: Iran Strait Restrictions Halt 49 Container Ships Bound for UAE

properties.title: Iran Strait Restrictions Halt 49 Container Ships Bound for UAE
properties.summary: Shipping data reveals that restrictions imposed by Iran's Revolutionary Guard in the Strait of Hormuz have blocked 49 container ships destined for the UAE's Jebel Ali port since February 28th. A total of 53 container ships have rerouted, with some heading to Oman and other UAE ports. The largest container shipping company, MSC, has suspended shipments to the Arabian Gulf, highlighting the critical impact on Dubai's import artery. This disruption occurs after Iran announced the closure of the strait and threatened to target American, European, and Israeli ships.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Key Global Oil, Gas Chokepoint Amid Tensions

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Key Global Oil, Gas Chokepoint Amid Tensions

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Key Global Oil, Gas Chokepoint Amid Tensions
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a vital global maritime chokepoint between Iran and Oman, facilitates the transit of approximately 20% of the world's crude oil and significant natural gas shipments. Recent escalations, including threats of closure by Iran in response to US sanctions and past military exercises, highlight its strategic importance. Incidents in the Persian Gulf, such as attacks on tankers and US withdrawal from the Iran nuclear deal, have increased tensions. Iran's development of an export terminal in the Sea of Oman offers an alternative route, bypassing the strait.

documents.title: Thai cargo ship hit near Strait of Hormuz, crew rescued

preview_img.title: Thai cargo ship hit near Strait of Hormuz, crew rescued

properties.title: Thai cargo ship hit near Strait of Hormuz, crew rescued
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai
properties.summary: Efforts are under way to recover the remaining three crew[BANGKOK] A Thai-flagged cargo vessel was struck near the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday (Mar 11), shortly after departing from the United Arab Emirates, the Royal Thai Navy said. The ship, Mayuree Naree, a 30,000 deadweight-tonne bulk carrier owned by Precious Shipping, was attacked while transiting the strategic waterway. Pictures shared by the Thai navy showed the ship billowing thick smoke from its rear section. Thai navy spokesperson Paraj Ratanajaipan said that Oman’s navy has rescued 20 crew members, who abandoned ship and evacuated in a lifeboat, and brought them ashore in Khasab. Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz – a vital chokepoint for global energy exports – has slowed sharply since the US-Israeli airstrikes on Iran triggered retaliatory actions, targeting shipping and regional infrastructure.

This strategic passage sees around a fifth of the oil consumed in the world, as well as significant quantities of liquefied natural gas (LNG). Read also American and Israeli strikes in Iran: Ahmed Ounaies fears a wider war Liquefied natural gas also affected The liquefied natural gas market has not been spared. Daily charter rates for LNG tankers jumped by more than 40% on Monday March 2, 2026, after Qatar announced the cessation of its production. The situation remains closely monitored by players in the sector, while the evolution of the conflict and the possible duration of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz remain uncertain.

This passage reflects Iran's policy of allowing transit for vessels from "non-hostile" countries. The Strait had been partially closed by Iran following U.S.-Israeli strikes in late February, but Iran later stated it would permit passage for ships unrelated to the U.S. or Israel. The development is closely watched by oil and commodity markets seeking signs of restored traffic in the vital waterway.

properties.title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Halting Japanese Shipping
properties.summary: Iran has ordered all ships to halt passage through the vital Strait of Hormuz, leading major Japanese shipping companies to temporarily suspend operations. This closure, occurring after reported air attacks by the U.S. and Israel on Iran, threatens to disrupt approximately 20% of the world's oil consumption and 11% of global trade. The strait, a critical chokepoint between Iran and Oman, sees around 3,000 vessels pass through monthly, carrying roughly 20 million barrels of oil per day. Japanese firms like Mitsui O.S.K. Lines have decided to stop navigation through the strait for safety reasons, impacting Japan's significant energy imports.

documents.title: Three tankers made it through the Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Three tankers made it through the Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Three tankers made it through the Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Three tankers sailing under the flag of Oman passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Friday, the traffic of which Iran has begun to block after coming under attack by the US and Israel. According to tracking data from shipping publication Lloyd's List, three Omani ships are moving in the Strait of Hormuz outside Iran's approved corridor near Larak Island, Al Jazeera news channel reported on Friday. On Friday, the US economic publication Bloomberg also announced the successful passage of a French container ship through the Strait of Hormuz, which is supposed to be the first of its kind since the beginning of the war. Citing shipping analytics firm Windward, Al Jazeera noted that more and more ships are passing through the Strait of Hormuz. About a fifth of the oil consumed in the world moves through the Strait of Hormuz, which connects the Persian Gulf to the world ocean, so the closure of the strait has caused great confusion in international trade and sharply increased prices.

documents.title: Drones Fall Near Dubai Airport, Ships Hit As Iran Presses On With Attacks

preview_img.title: Drones Fall Near Dubai Airport, Ships Hit As Iran Presses On With Attacks

properties.title: Drones Fall Near Dubai Airport, Ships Hit As Iran Presses On With Attacks
properties.summary: Drones fell near Dubai airport, injuring four people, while ships were hit in or near the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday as Iran kept up its campaign of disrupting oil markets and air and maritime traffic. Iran has also targeted Gulf energy infrastructure and choked shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, which normally carries nearly 20 per cent of global oil production, prompting wild swings in prices. A third ship was also hit by a projectile off Oman, in the Strait of Hormuz, which caused a fire that was later extinguished according to the UKMTO. Saudi Arabia intercepted drones targeting the Shaybah field, crucial to its production, while explosions rang out over Qatar and the UAE reported fresh attacks. Iranian attacks have already forced state-owned QatarEnergy, one of the world’s largest producers of liquefied natural gas, to halt production last week and declare force majeure.

documents.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Shipping, Strikes UAE

preview_img.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Shipping, Strikes UAE

properties.title: Iran Threatens Strait of Hormuz Shipping, Strikes UAE
properties.summary: Iran has effectively declared the Strait of Hormuz impassable, threatening to burn ships that attempt to transit the critical waterway, which handles about a fifth of global oil consumption. This escalation has led to significant disruptions in shipping, with tanker traffic reportedly dropping dramatically. Iran's actions have extended beyond the strait, with reported strikes impacting the UAE, including Dubai, shattering its image as a safe haven and causing significant economic repercussions.

documents.title: Three Ships Cross Strait of Hormuz Off Oman Coast Amid Tensions

preview_img.title: Three Ships Cross Strait of Hormuz Off Oman Coast Amid Tensions

properties.title: Three Ships Cross Strait of Hormuz Off Oman Coast Amid Tensions
properties.summary: Three ships, two oil tankers and a natural gas vessel, crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, marking the first non-Iranian-approved transit in nearly three weeks. The vessels are currently located off the coast of Muscat, Oman. This passage occurred despite Iranian attacks in retaliation for U.S. and Israeli actions, which had previously paralyzed maritime traffic and caused a surge in global oil and gas prices. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global energy markets, with around one-fifth of the world's crude oil and natural gas normally transiting through it.

documents.title: 3 Cargo Ships Hit by Unknown Projectiles in Strategic Waterways, Including Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: 3 Cargo Ships Hit by Unknown Projectiles in Strategic Waterways, Including Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: 3 Cargo Ships Hit by Unknown Projectiles in Strategic Waterways, Including Strait of Hormuz
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai
properties.summary: Three cargo ships were struck by unidentified projectiles in regional shipping lanes on Wednesday, including the Strait of Hormuz, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations. One vessel suffered a fire in the Strait of Hormuz, approximately 11 nautical miles north of Oman, but the fire was later controlled. Another ship, a bulk carrier, was hit 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai, with its crew unharmed and an investigation underway. A Thai shipping vessel also reported an attack near the Strait of Hormuz, with ongoing rescue operations for three crew members.

Another ship, a bulk carrier, was hit 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai, with its crew unharmed and an investigation underway. A Thai shipping vessel also reported an attack near the Strait of Hormuz, with ongoing rescue operations for three crew members.

documents.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions

preview_img.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions

properties.title: French Ship Transits Strait of Hormuz Amid Regional Tensions
properties.summary: A French-flagged container ship, the CMA CGM Kribi, has transited the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such passage by a French vessel since the start of the current conflict. The ship departed Dubai on Thursday, heading towards Iran and transmitting its French ownership details. It navigated close to the Iranian coast before reaching the vicinity of Muscat, Oman on Friday morning. The transit occurred amidst heightened regional tensions, with Bloomberg confirming the passage through its tracking data and sources familiar with the matter.

documents.title: Strait of Hormuz Shipping Traffic Expected to Gradually Resume

preview_img.title: Strait of Hormuz Shipping Traffic Expected to Gradually Resume

properties.title: Strait of Hormuz Shipping Traffic Expected to Gradually Resume
properties.summary: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is showing signs of a gradual increase, with 16 freighters passing through on Wednesday, up from 11 the previous day, according to data firm Windward. While numbers are still a fraction of pre-war levels, a French container ship belonging to CMA CGM also transited the strait on Thursday, its passage through the Iran-controlled section remaining unexplained. The Iranian government has announced plans for a new mechanism to regulate shipping in coordination with Oman, aiming to facilitate safe passage, though specific details have not been released. This comes amid reports of demands for payment for safe passage and six Hapag-Lloyd ships currently stranded in the Persian Gulf.

documents.title: Iran Allows Ships Through Hormuz Strait After Easing Tensions

preview_img.title: Iran Allows Ships Through Hormuz Strait After Easing Tensions

properties.title: Iran Allows Ships Through Hormuz Strait After Easing Tensions
properties.summary: Several vessels from Oman, France, and Japan have successfully navigated the Strait of Hormuz since Thursday, according to Reuters. This follows Iran's decision to permit passage for ships deemed friendly, after initially closing the crucial waterway following U.S. and Israeli strikes in late February. The resumption of traffic, though intermittent, is closely watched by global oil and commodity markets. China also confirmed three of its ships recently transited the strait after coordinating with relevant parties, emphasizing the need for peace and stability in the region.

documents.title: Iran and Oman drafting protocol to co-monitor Hormuz Strait traffic: Iranian news agency - Region - World

preview_img.title: Iran and Oman drafting protocol to co-monitor Hormuz Strait traffic: Iranian news agency - Region - World

properties.title: Iran and Oman drafting protocol to co-monitor Hormuz Strait traffic: Iranian news agency - Region - World
properties.summary: Iran and Oman are drafting a protocol to monitor ship transit through the Strait of Hormuz, Iranian state news agency IRNA reported on Thursday, citing Kazem Gharibabadi, Iran’s deputy minister of legal and international affairs. The announcement also follows approval by an Iranian parliamentary commission on Monday of plans to impose tolls on vessels transiting the strait, according to State TV. Oman and Iran are the two countries that overlook the Strait of Hormuz, the key international waterway which normally carries around a fifth of global crude oil and liquefied natural gas. Iran has said "non-hostile vessels" may transit the strait if they comply with safety and security regulations coordinated with the relevant authorities. It has allowed tankers from countries that oppose the war or refused to join the aggression, including Japan, China, India, and Pakistan, to pass through Hormuz.

preview_img.title: French Container Ship First Western Vessel Through Strait of Hormuz Since War

properties.title: French Container Ship First Western Vessel Through Strait of Hormuz Since War
properties.summary: A French container ship successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, marking the first passage by a Western European vessel since tensions escalated. The CMA CGM-operated ship, identified as having a French owner before entering Iranian waters, navigated the critical waterway that Iran has largely blocked to ships linked to the U.S. and Israel. This event occurs as France advocates for diplomatic solutions over military action in the region. Iran also announced it is drafting a maritime safety agreement with Oman, potentially involving transit fees for vessels.

documents.title: Five ships transit Hormuz Strait amid Iran's shipping policy

preview_img.title: Five ships transit Hormuz Strait amid Iran's shipping policy

properties.title: Five ships transit Hormuz Strait amid Iran's shipping policy
properties.summary: At least five ships, including three Omani-managed tankers, a French container ship, and a Japanese LNG carrier, transited the Strait of Hormuz starting Thursday, according to maritime data. This transit reflects Iran's policy of allowing passage to vessels from "non-hostile" countries following an earlier closure. The reopening comes as oil and commodity markets anxiously seek signs of restored traffic in the vital waterway. A French CMA CGM container ship and Omani tankers were among those passing through, with a Japanese LNG tanker being the first Japanese vessel and first LNG tanker to exit the strait since the conflict began.

documents.title: Tankers Breach Hormuz Strait Blockade Amid Diplomatic Talks

preview_img.title: Tankers Breach Hormuz Strait Blockade Amid Diplomatic Talks

properties.title: Tankers Breach Hormuz Strait Blockade Amid Diplomatic Talks
properties.summary: Two oil tankers and an LNG carrier sailed east through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, according to their satellite signals. All three vessels are managed by Oman Ship Management Company, which was unavailable for comment. Iran has been negotiating passage for ships from allied nations via a northern route through its waters, while also discussing a traffic monitoring protocol with Oman. The passage of these large vessels, particularly tankers carrying two million barrels of crude oil each, marks a significant development following the de facto closure of the vital waterway that has disrupted global energy markets.

documents.title: Iran to Implement New Passage Regime in Strait of Hormuz, Sparking Trade Concerns

preview_img.title: Iran to Implement New Passage Regime in Strait of Hormuz, Sparking Trade Concerns

properties.title: Iran to Implement New Passage Regime in Strait of Hormuz, Sparking Trade Concerns
properties.summary: Iran plans to introduce a new passage regime in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global shipping lane, potentially impacting international trade and security. The proposed protocol, developed with Oman, will cover procedures for military and commercial vessels, with Iran and Oman designated to ensure safe passage. While details on specific tariffs are premature, the move follows recent escalations between Iran, the U.S., and Israel, which had previously led to a de facto blockade of the strait. The initiative aims to define responsibilities for flag states and vessel owners regarding passage services.

properties.title: 3 Fuel Ships Pass Through Strait of Hormuz Outside Iranian Corridor
properties.summary: Three fuel ships, including two oil tankers and one LNG carrier, have successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz without using a corridor imposed by Iran, according to maritime intelligence firms. This marks the first time in three weeks that vessels have bypassed the route established by Iran's Revolutionary Guard, which has been criticized by Western and Arab nations as "extortion." The ships, reportedly flagged as Omani, navigated through the Omani side of the strait. The incidents occur amidst heightened regional tensions and a significant drop in maritime traffic through the vital waterway.

preview_img.title: Traffic Jam Outside the Strait of Hormuz Amid Uncertainty Over Israel-Iran Truce

properties.title: Traffic Jam Outside the Strait of Hormuz Amid Uncertainty Over Israel-Iran Truce
properties.summary: A traffic jam has formed outside the Strait of Hormuz as uncertainty over the Israel-Iran truce increases the number of ships waiting to safely move towards the Persian Gulf. Dozens of tankers, container ships, and cargo ships are waiting near the coasts of Oman and the United Arab Emirates to pass through the Strait. According to shipping circles, there is a slow down in crossings, as convoys need to be created with the protection of warships. However, there is no significant reduction in the transported cargo.

documents.title: Government prepares port safeguards amid Hormuz Strait's shipping risks, says Transport Minister

preview_img.title: Government prepares port safeguards amid Hormuz Strait's shipping risks, says Transport Minister

properties.title: Government prepares port safeguards amid Hormuz Strait's shipping risks, says Transport Minister
properties.summary: Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the matter was discussed in a meeting this morning with port management, shipping companies and national maritime industry authorities to plan immediate steps to address possible disruptions to the global logistics chain. The Strait of Hormuz, located between Iran and Oman, is one of the world’s most important oil shipping routes, with about one-fifth of global oil consumption passing through the narrow waterway each day. Loke said that as an initial measure, the government will facilitate the removal of empty containers from port areas to prevent congestion, while port management will carry out more thorough inspections. The move is intended to ensure that containers with unclear destinations are not unloaded at the country’s ports, he explained. "In facing a national crisis, all stakeholders must work together and support one another to protect the nation’s interests,” he added.

properties.title: Iran Restricts Strait of Hormuz Traffic Amid Tensions; Some Nations Gain Passage
properties.summary: Iran is imposing targeted restrictions on maritime traffic through the strategic Strait of Hormuz amidst escalating tensions with the U.S. and Israel. While officially stating that ships from "non-hostile" countries can pass, only a few nations like Pakistan, India, and Turkey have confirmed receiving specific authorizations for their vessels. China, France, and Italy are actively negotiating with Tehran to secure safe passage for their oil and gas shipments. The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical chokepoint for global trade, significantly impacting energy markets and international economic stability.

documents.title: Oman urges fishermen to avoid shipping lanes

properties.title: Oman urges fishermen to avoid shipping lanes
properties.summary: Muscat: The Ministry of Transport, Communications, and Information Technology (MTCIT) has warned fishermen and seagoers to avoid fishing in shipping lanes. It urged everyone to adhere to the instructions to avoid legal consequences. It may be noted that the Hormuz Strait is wedged between Iran and Oman and links the Sea of Oman and the Arabian Sea. As the main shipping route for oil from the Middle East, the strait sees a fifth of the world’s oil consumption passing through it daily. To help manage capacity and avoid collisions, the strait operates a two-lane traffic system, in which inbound vessels use one lane and outbound vessels use the other.

It urged everyone to adhere to the instructions to avoid legal consequences. It may be noted that the Hormuz Strait is wedged between Iran and Oman and links the Sea of Oman and the Arabian Sea. As the main shipping route for oil from the Middle East, the strait sees a fifth of the world’s oil consumption passing through it daily. To help manage capacity and avoid collisions, the strait operates a two-lane traffic system, in which inbound vessels use one lane and outbound vessels use the other.

documents.title: Cargo Ships Advised to Avoid Iranian Waters in Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran-Israel Tensions

preview_img.title: Cargo Ships Advised to Avoid Iranian Waters in Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran-Israel Tensions

properties.title: Cargo Ships Advised to Avoid Iranian Waters in Strait of Hormuz Amid Iran-Israel Tensions
properties.summary: Cargo ships are avoiding Iranian waters near the Strait of Hormuz, sailing closer to Oman's coast due to the risk of conflict between Iran and Israel. Electronic interference and rising tanker charter costs are increasing concerns about maritime safety and stability. International observers warn that disruptions to this strategic route could affect global trade and oil prices. Shipping routes have been diverted to international waters in the Gulf of Oman to minimize the risk of attacks or interference.

documents.title: Iran Attacks Thai Cargo Ship in Strait of Hormuz; 3 Crew Missing

preview_img.title: Iran Attacks Thai Cargo Ship in Strait of Hormuz; 3 Crew Missing

properties.title: Iran Attacks Thai Cargo Ship in Strait of Hormuz; 3 Crew Missing
properties.summary: Iran attacked a Thai cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, setting it ablaze and causing three crew members to go missing. Twenty crew members were rescued by the Omani navy. Maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has been disrupted since Feb. 28 due to conflict between the U.S., Israel, and Iran. This incident is one of at least 12 confirmed incidents involving ships in the area since the war began, with at least seven sailors killed.

documents.title: GPS Interference in Strait of Hormuz Affects Nearly 1,000 Vessels

preview_img.title: GPS Interference in Strait of Hormuz Affects Nearly 1,000 Vessels

properties.title: GPS Interference in Strait of Hormuz Affects Nearly 1,000 Vessels
properties.summary: From June 15 to June 18, approximately 970 ships per day experienced GPS interference in the Persian Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz. The interference, which began with the latest surge in Middle East tensions, has caused ships' AIS signals to appear in unexpected locations, such as Iranian ports and the Omani desert. The Strait of Hormuz remains open for commercial traffic, but the risks of passage have increased, leading to delays and changes in routing strategies. The interference peaked on Tuesday, affecting 1,155 vessels.

documents.title: Iran tells UN: 'Non-hostile' ships can transit Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran tells UN: 'Non-hostile' ships can transit Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran tells UN: 'Non-hostile' ships can transit Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: For context, always refer to the full article. A cargo ship in the Gulf, near the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from northern Ras al-Khaimah, near the border with Oman’s Musandam governance, amid the US-Israeli conflict with Iran, in United Arab Emirates, March 11, 2026. The US-Israeli war against Iran has effectively halted shipments of about one-fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas through the strait, disrupting global oil supply. The note from Iran’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs was sent to the 15-member Security Council and UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres on Sunday, March 22. It was then circulated on Tuesday among the 176 members of the London-based UN shipping agency responsible for regulating international shipping safety, security, and pollution prevention.

documents.title: Ships Targeted in Persian Gulf Since Start of Iran War

preview_img.title: Ships Targeted in Persian Gulf Since Start of Iran War

properties.title: Ships Targeted in Persian Gulf Since Start of Iran War
properties.summary: The war between the U.S./Israel and Iran has disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for oil and LNG. Iran has warned that any ship transiting the narrow strait will be a target. Since February 28, multiple vessels have been struck, including oil tankers and container ships, resulting in crew casualties and fires. Attacks have occurred in various locations, including off the coasts of Oman, the UAE, and near Bahrain, Iraq, and Saudi Arabia. The incidents have led to port closures and raised concerns about international shipping security.

documents.title: Iran Threatens to Block Red Sea Shipping Lanes After Hormuz Strait Tolls

preview_img.title: Iran Threatens to Block Red Sea Shipping Lanes After Hormuz Strait Tolls

properties.title: Iran Threatens to Block Red Sea Shipping Lanes After Hormuz Strait Tolls
properties.summary: Iran is escalating threats by proposing tolls for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz and warning of potential blockades of the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait at the entrance to the Red Sea. The Iranian parliament is considering a law to charge $2 million per vessel for passage through Hormuz, potentially generating billions. This move aims to secure funds for war expenses and counter potential military actions. A blockade of the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait would significantly disrupt global trade, impacting roughly 12% of world trade volume and 10% of oil shipments, effectively closing off alternative routes from Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Oman.

documents.title: US Warns US-flagged Ships to Avoid Iranian Waters

preview_img.title: US Warns US-flagged Ships to Avoid Iranian Waters

properties.title: US Warns US-flagged Ships to Avoid Iranian Waters
properties.summary: The U.S. Maritime Administration has issued a security advisory for U.S.-flagged commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz. Ships are urged to maintain maximum distance from Iranian waters and favor routes near Omani waters, especially when traveling east. The advisory, effective until August, follows an incident in February where Iran attempted to seize a U.S.-flagged vessel. Captains are advised to deny Iranian boarding requests and keep their Automatic Identification System (AIS) active.

documents.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage in Strait of Hormuz Amid U.S. Blockade

preview_img.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage in Strait of Hormuz Amid U.S. Blockade

properties.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage in Strait of Hormuz Amid U.S. Blockade
properties.summary: Iran has proposed a safe shipping corridor in the Strait of Hormuz, located in Omani waters, as a solution to resume commercial traffic. This initiative comes in response to a blockade imposed by the U.S. Navy on Iranian ports. The proposal, presented to the U.S. as part of peace negotiations, aims to allow commercial vessels free passage on the Omani side of the strait. The specifics regarding clearing potential naval mines in the area remain unclear, and the offer is conditional on Washington's acceptance of Tehran's demands. The ongoing conflict has disrupted global oil and gas supplies, with hundreds of tankers and approximately 20,000 sailors reportedly stranded.

documents.title: Russian Billionaire's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Despite Restrictions

preview_img.title: Russian Billionaire's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Despite Restrictions

properties.title: Russian Billionaire's Yacht Transits Strait of Hormuz Despite Restrictions
properties.summary: The superyacht 'Nord,' belonging to Russia's wealthiest man, Aleksei Mordashov, traversed the Strait of Hormuz on April 24, despite Iran's official restrictions on maritime traffic. The 142-meter vessel departed Dubai and headed towards Oman, passing through the strategic waterway during the night. Iranian authorities reportedly turned a blind eye, allowing limited passage for certain vessels, including those linked to sanctioned firms. This incident raises questions about the selectivity of regional maritime regulations amid heightened tensions between the U.S., Israel, and Iran.

documents.title: Hormuz blockade sees alternative land routes opening for trade - Breaking News

preview_img.title: Hormuz blockade sees alternative land routes opening for trade - Breaking News

properties.title: Hormuz blockade sees alternative land routes opening for trade - Breaking News
properties.summary: Mirror AI Summary - Quick Read Genarated by Mirror AI MIRROR AI Hide Loading… AI SUMMARY GENERATED ONLY FROM THIS ARTICLE’S TEXT. Al Jazeera - The closure of the Strait of Hormuz is reshaping trade routes with container ship traffic now using land routes to reach destinations, the AFP news agency reports. The blockade of the strait has pushed ship owners to find alternative land corridors to deliver foodstuffs and manufactured goods by truck, as they can no longer reach the Gulf’s coastal countries by sea, according to logistics and maritime sources. “The port of Jeddah is not at all sized to handle such import volumes and a port congestion situation is emerging,” said Arthur Barillas de The, cofounder of freight forwarder Ovrsea. Shipowners have said they will also use three ports outside the Strait of Hormuz – Oman’s Sohar, and the UAE ports of Khor Fakkan and Fujairah.

properties.summary: GLOBAL MARITIME: "𝗙𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗼𝗳 𝗻𝗮𝘃𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲": 𝗜𝗠𝗢 𝗮𝗱𝗱𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗨𝗡 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗹 𝗼𝗻 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗮𝗳𝗲𝘁𝘆 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗛𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘂𝘇 IMO Secretary-General Mr. Arsenio Dominguez has called on States to uphold the principle of freedom of navigation and reject any imposition of tolls, fees or discriminatory transit measures for passage through straits used for international navigation. Addressing the UN Security Council on the topic of 'The safety and protection of waterways in the maritime domain, he emphasized:"The principle of freedom of navigation is not negotiable. Ships must be allowed to trade worldwide unhindered and in accordance with international law." Any deviation from these well-established and recognized principles would set a negative precedent and severely undermine the integrity and stability of shipping operations worldwide, he added. He urged States to support the IMO’s plan to facilitate the evacuation of affected seafarers and, at the appropriate time, to assist with the clearance of any hazards to the commercial ships navigating in the Strait of Hormuz, including mines. Read his full statement is below: 𝑼𝑵 𝑺𝒆𝒄𝒖𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒚 𝑪𝒐𝒖𝒏𝒄𝒊𝒍 𝑯𝒊𝒈𝒉-𝑳𝒆𝒗𝒆𝒍 𝑶𝒑𝒆𝒏 𝑫𝒆𝒃𝒂𝒕𝒆 “𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑺𝒂𝒇𝒆𝒕𝒚 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒕𝒆𝒄𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏 𝒐𝒇 𝑾𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒘𝒂𝒚𝒔 𝒊𝒏 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝑴𝒂𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒎𝒆 𝑫𝒐𝒎𝒂𝒊𝒏” 𝑨𝒈𝒆𝒏𝒅𝒂 𝒊𝒕𝒆𝒎 “𝑴𝒂𝒊𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒏𝒂𝒏𝒄𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝒊𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒏𝒂𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏𝒂𝒍 𝒑𝒆𝒂𝒄𝒆 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒔𝒆𝒄𝒖𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒚” 𝑲𝒆𝒚𝒏𝒐𝒕𝒆 𝑹𝒆𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒌𝒔 𝒃𝒚 𝑰𝑴𝑶 𝑺𝒆𝒄𝒓𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒓𝒚-𝑮𝒆𝒏𝒆𝒓𝒂𝒍 𝑨𝒓𝒔𝒆𝒏𝒊𝒐 𝑫𝒐𝒎𝒊𝒏𝒈𝒖𝒆𝒛 𝑴𝒐𝒏𝒅𝒂𝒚, 27 𝑨𝒑𝒓𝒊𝒍 2026 𝒂𝒕 4 𝒑.𝒎. (11 𝒂.𝒎. 𝑵𝒀 𝒕𝒊𝒎𝒆) Thank you, Mr. President, and distinguished members of the United Nations Security Council. Since the 1980’s, IMO has been addressing maritime security in the shipping and port sector and has in place the relevant mandatory standards to enhance global maritime security. Our regulatory process is constantly evolving to adapt to any new threats and make use of our experience and expertise. The geopolitical conflict around the Strait of Hormuz is having a very negative effect on seafarers and shipping, and on the global population and economy. Approximately, 20,000 innocent seafarers and nearly 2,000 vessels remained trapped in the Persian Gulf. Any disruption to shipping represents a global interference to energy and food security. The principle of freedom of navigation is not negotiable. Ships must be allowed to trade worldwide unhindered and in accordance with international law. In accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and customary international law: • Straits used for international navigation cannot be closed by bordering States. • Equally, there is no legal basis for any country to introduce payments or impose tolls, fees or any discriminatory conditions on international straits. Any deviation from these well-established and recognized principles would set a negative precedent and severely undermine the integrity and stability of shipping operations worldwide. Since 1968, the International Maritime Organization has established a traffic separation scheme or maritime corridor, in the Strait of Hormuz. This maritime corridor, jointly operated between Oman and Iran, has ensured safe navigation for decades. This is a mandatory mechanism in accordance with the IMO’s International Convention on the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS). Both countries are signatories to this Convention, which has governed the safety of ships at sea since 1974. Our main concern remains the safety and the well-being of the innocent seafarers trapped in the Gulf, due to the conflict. Following the IMO Council decision, I have begun the development of an evacuation framework, using the existing traffic separation scheme, to securely evacuate ships and seafarers from the conflict zone provided it is safe to do so. The technical and operational developments are being carried out with the involvement of the regional countries, including Iran, and with the support from relevant countries assisting in the negotiations. As an Organization, we are ready to implement this framework without delay, but only once it is safe to do so. In this respect, I call upon States to uphold the principle of freedom of navigation; and reject an imposition of tolls, fees or discriminatory transit measures for the passage through a strait used for international navigation. I would like to take this opportunity to request States to: • Support the IMO’s plan to facilitate the evacuation of affected seafarers; • At the appropriate time, to assist with the clearance of any hazards to the commercial ships navigating in the Strait of Hormuz, including mines; • Provide any technical and operational civilian assistance to operationalize the evacuation framework; and • Ensure practical assurances to the shipping sector, including access to in...

Any deviation from these well-established and recognized principles would set a negative precedent and severely undermine the integrity and stability of shipping operations worldwide, he added. He urged States to support the IMO’s plan to facilitate the evacuation of affected seafarers and, at the appropriate time, to assist with the clearance of any hazards to the commercial ships navigating in the Strait of Hormuz, including mines. Read his full statement is below: 𝑼𝑵 𝑺𝒆𝒄𝒖𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒚 𝑪𝒐𝒖𝒏𝒄𝒊𝒍 𝑯𝒊𝒈𝒉-𝑳𝒆𝒗𝒆𝒍 𝑶𝒑𝒆𝒏 𝑫𝒆𝒃𝒂𝒕𝒆 “𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑺𝒂𝒇𝒆𝒕𝒚 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒕𝒆𝒄𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏 𝒐𝒇 𝑾𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒘𝒂𝒚𝒔 𝒊𝒏 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝑴𝒂𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒎𝒆 𝑫𝒐𝒎𝒂𝒊𝒏” 𝑨𝒈𝒆𝒏𝒅𝒂 𝒊𝒕𝒆𝒎 “𝑴𝒂𝒊𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒏𝒂𝒏𝒄𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝒊𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒏𝒂𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏𝒂𝒍 𝒑𝒆𝒂𝒄𝒆 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒔𝒆𝒄𝒖𝒓𝒊𝒕𝒚” 𝑲𝒆𝒚𝒏𝒐𝒕𝒆 𝑹𝒆𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒌𝒔 𝒃𝒚 𝑰𝑴𝑶 𝑺𝒆𝒄𝒓𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒓𝒚-𝑮𝒆𝒏𝒆𝒓𝒂𝒍 𝑨𝒓𝒔𝒆𝒏𝒊𝒐 𝑫𝒐𝒎𝒊𝒏𝒈𝒖𝒆𝒛 𝑴𝒐𝒏𝒅𝒂𝒚, 27 𝑨𝒑𝒓𝒊𝒍 2026 𝒂𝒕 4 𝒑.𝒎. (11 𝒂.𝒎. 𝑵𝒀 𝒕𝒊𝒎𝒆) Thank you, Mr. President, and distinguished members of the United Nations Security Council. Since the 1980’s, IMO has been addressing maritime security in the shipping and port sector and has in place the relevant mandatory standards to enhance global maritime security. Our regulatory process is constantly evolving to adapt to any new threats and make use of our experience and expertise. The geopolitical conflict around the Strait of Hormuz is having a very negative effect on seafarers and shipping, and on the global population and economy. Approximately, 20,000 innocent seafarers and nearly 2,000 vessels remained trapped in the Persian Gulf. Any disruption to shipping represents a global interference to energy and food security. The principle of freedom of navigation is not negotiable. Ships must be allowed to trade worldwide unhindered and in accordance with international law. In accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and customary international law: • Straits used for international navigation cannot be closed by bordering States. • Equally, there is no legal basis for any country to introduce payments or impose tolls, fees or any discriminatory conditions on international straits. Any deviation from these well-established and recognized principles would set a negative precedent and severely undermine the integrity and stability of shipping operations worldwide. Since 1968, the International Maritime Organization has established a traffic separation scheme or maritime corridor, in the Strait of Hormuz. This maritime corridor, jointly operated between Oman and Iran, has ensured safe navigation for decades. This is a mandatory mechanism in accordance with the IMO’s International Convention on the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS). Both countries are signatories to this Convention, which has governed the safety of ships at sea since 1974. Our main concern remains the safety and the well-being of the innocent seafarers trapped in the Gulf, due to the conflict. Following the IMO Council decision, I have begun the development of an evacuation framework, using the existing traffic separation scheme, to securely evacuate ships and seafarers from the conflict zone provided it is safe to do so. The technical and operational developments are being carried out with the involvement of the regional countries, including Iran, and with the support from relevant countries assisting in the negotiations. As an Organization, we are ready to implement this framework without delay, but only once it is safe to do so. In this respect, I call upon States to uphold the principle of freedom of navigation; and reject an imposition of tolls, fees or discriminatory transit measures for the passage through a strait used for international navigation. I would like to take this opportunity to request States to: • Support the IMO’s plan to facilitate the evacuation of affected seafarers; • At the appropriate time, to assist with the clearance of any hazards to the commercial ships navigating in the Strait of Hormuz, including mines; • Provide any technical and operational civilian assistance to operationalize the evacuation framework; and • Ensure practical assurances to the shipping sector, including access to in...

documents.title: China Urges Protection for Ships in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

preview_img.title: China Urges Protection for Ships in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions

properties.title: China Urges Protection for Ships in Strait of Hormuz Amid Tensions
properties.summary: China has called for all parties to protect ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz as tensions escalate in the Middle East. The crucial waterway, linking the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman, saw shipping traffic drop by 60% after Iran retaliated with blockades following U.S. and Israeli missile attacks. Beijing urged an immediate cessation of military activities to prevent further escalation and ensure maritime security. The Strait of Hormuz is a vital global trade artery, handling about 20% of the world's oil and LNG exports, and disruptions severely impact major importers like China and India.

documents.title: The U.S. has already walked back President Donald Trumps threats of blocking the entire strait, instead focusing on ship...

preview_img.title: The U.S. has already walked back President Donald Trumps threats of blocking the entire strait, instead focusing on ship...

properties.title: The U.S. has already walked back President Donald Trumps threats of blocking the entire strait, instead focusing on ship...
properties.summary: The U.S. has already walked back President Donald Trumps threats of blocking the entire strait, instead focusing on ships leaving Iranian ports. CAIRO, Egypt - The U.S. military announced it will begin a blockade of all Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, as President Donald Trump sought to pressure Iran in a move that risks driving oil prices even higher and renewing the war. The announcement set the stage for a showdown, as Iran immediately responded with threats on all ports in the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman. Security in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman is either for everyone or for NO ONE, the Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting reported Monday. NO PORT in the region will be safe, according to a statement from the Iranian military and the Revolutionary Guards. U.S. Central Command announced the blockade would begin on Monday at 10 a.m. EDT, or 5:30 p.m. in Iran, and would be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf (Persian Gulf) and Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM said it would still allow ships traveling between non-Iranian ports to transit the strait, a step down from the presidents earlier threat to blockade the entire strait. The announcement of the blockade halted the limited ship traffic that resumed in the strait since the ceasefire, said an early report from Lloyds List intelligence. Marine trackers say over 40 commercial ships have crossed since the start of the ceasefire, down from roughly 100 to 135 vessel passages per day before the war. The move came after marathon U.S.-Iran ceasefire talks in Pakistan ended without an agreement on Saturday. U.S. Vice President JD Vance said the talks stalled after Iran refused to accept American terms to refrain from developing a nuclear weapon. Iran has demanded compensation for damage caused by U.S.-Israeli strikes that launched the war on Feb. 28, and the release of Irans frozen assets. Later Sunday, Trump extended his feud over the war with Pope Leo XIV, lashing out in a Truth Social post that c #localmemphis   #crimewatchmemphis alled the Catholic leader terrible on foreign policy. The extraordinary broadside came after Leo denounced the war and demanded that political leaders stop and negotiate peace. The blockade could have far-reaching effects The blockade is likely intended to add pressure on Iran, which has exported millions of barrels of oil since the war began, much of it likely carried by so-called dark transits that evade Western government sanctions and oversight. Trump also hopes to undercut Irans control over the Strait of Hormuz after demanding that it reopen the waterway where 20%% of global oil transited before fighting began. A U.S. blockade could further rattle global energy markets. Oil prices rose in early market trading after the blockade announcement. The price of U.S. crude rose 8%% to $104.24 a barrel, and Brent crude oil, the international standard, rose 7%% to $102.29. Brent crude cost roughly $70 per barrel before the war in late February. Prime Minister Keir Starmer told BBC radio Monday that Britain will not be part of a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports in response to the closing of the Strait of Hormuz and that Britain is not getting dragged into the war. He said U.K. efforts remain focused on reopening the key shipping route, and that Britain might help with mine clearing in the waterway, but only after the fighting stops. Iran says  if you fight, we will fight A chorus of top-ranking Iranian officials threatened retaliation. Mohsen Rezaei, a military adviser and a former Revolutionary Guard Commander, wrote on X that the countrys armed forces had major untouched levers to counter a Hormuz blockade. He said Iran would not be coerced by tweets and imaginary plans. Iranian parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, who led Irans side in the talks, addressed Trump in a statement on his return to Iran: If you fight, we will fight. Irans Revolutionary Guard later said the strait remained under Irans full control and was open for non-military vessels, but military ones would get a forceful response, two semiofficial Iranian news agencies reported. During the 21-hour talks this weekend in Pakistan, the U.S. military said two destroyers had transited the strait ahead of mine-clearing work, a first since the war began. Iran denied it. No word on what happens after ceasefire expires The face-to-face talks that ended early Sunday were the highest-level negotiations https://ift.tt/yspL3N0

documents.title: Pakistan plans to export surplus food supplies to the Arab Gulf states as food security concerns mount due to Iran's con...

preview_img.title: Pakistan plans to export surplus food supplies to the Arab Gulf states as food security concerns mount due to Iran's con...

properties.title: Pakistan plans to export surplus food supplies to the Arab Gulf states as food security concerns mount due to Iran's con...
properties.summary: Pakistan plans to export surplus food supplies to the Arab Gulf states as food security concerns mount due to Iran's control of the strategic Strait of Hormuz. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif on Wednesday directed authorities to accelerate the export of surplus food items to Gulf countries while ensuring an uninterrupted domestic supply, according to a press handout issued by his office. The statement added that Islamabad is looking to expand flight operations and enhance port efficiency as a result of the US-Israeli war on Iran. Iran has wrested control of the Strait of Hormuz and is imposing its own corridor system. Traffic through the waterway has plunged, while Iran allows a select number of vessels to transit, in some cases levying $2m fees, according to the maritime intelligence publication, Lloyd's List. While Hormuz is a chokepoint for 20 percent of the world’s energy, it’s also a vital passageway for Gulf food supplies. Some countries, such as Saudi Arabia, which has access to the Red Sea, are less affected by this closure. But for the UAE, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, it is critical. Roughly 90 percent of the UAE’s food supplies traverse the waterway. Experts previously warned Middle East Eye that Gulf countries could face a reckoning in food imports if the Strait of Hormuz remained closed to them and western vessels. “Increased shipping and insurance costs will all manifest themselves in food inflation,” Christian Henderson, a scholar of political economy and food security at Leiden University, told MEE. Pakistan has been one of the few countries to safely navigate the Strait of Hormuz. Earlier this month, the Lorax, a Pakistan-flagged ship also known as the Karachi, became the first vessel carrying non-Iranian crude to transit through Hormuz, with its ship-tracking data, called the Automatic Identification System, on. Pakistan has been walking a tightrope as it tries to manage its ties to its neighbour Iran and important Gulf partners that have long served as its financial lifeline. The country is also among the most impacted by the plunge in Gulf energy exports. Fuel rationing has already begun, including a four-day working week for government employees and a two-week closure of schools.

documents.title: No Safe Transit Through Hormuz, Warns IMO Chief; 20,000 Seafarers Stranded @IMOHQ  @IMOSecGen  @nbrownIMOThe Internation

properties.title: No Safe Transit Through Hormuz, Warns IMO Chief; 20,000 Seafarers Stranded @IMOHQ  @IMOSecGen  @nbrownIMOThe Internation
properties.summary: No Safe Transit Through Hormuz, Warns IMO Chief; 20,000 Seafarers Stranded @IMOHQ  @IMOSecGen  @nbrownIMOThe International Maritime Organization has raised serious alarm over the worsening security situation in the Strait of Hormuz, with Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez declaring that “there is no safe transit anywhere” in the region.Addressing Member States and industry stakeholders, Dominguez revealed that nearly 20,000 seafarers aboard around 1,600 vessels remain stranded in the Persian Gulf amid escalating tensions. Several ships have reportedly been seized or detained, while threats from naval mines and continued attacks have made navigation extremely hazardous.Since the start of the conflict, the IMO has verified 29 attacks on vessels, leading to the deaths of at least 10 seafarers and significant damage to ships.Seafarers Facing Humanitarian CrisisWith the crisis entering its eighth week, concerns are mounting over dwindling onboard supplies, including food, water, and fuel. Dominguez highlighted the psychological toll on crews, sharing a firsthand account from a stranded seafarer who described feeling “invisible” and neglected.He urged governments, NGOs, and industry bodies to intensify support through remote assistance, helplines, and family communication, while also stressing the importance of fair wages and humane treatment.Call for Immediate Release and Protection“The seafarers are not at fault,” Dominguez emphasized, calling for their immediate release and stronger international coordination to ensure their safety.Evacuation Plan Under PreparationThe IMO is working with global partners on a maritime evacuation plan, which will be activated once security conditions permit. The plan includes identifying and prioritizing vessels based on humanitarian needs.Dominguez noted that safe evacuation would require all parties in the conflict to halt attacks during the operation. The internationally recognized Traffic Separation Scheme (TSS) adopted by the IMO in 1968 has been proposed as a potential evacuation corridor.The IMO is also coordinating with Iran and Oman, along with support from countries including France and the United Kingdom.UN Briefing ScheduledThe situation is set to be escalated further, with the IMO scheduled to brief the United Nations Security Council on 27 April 2026.The crisis underscores a growing humanitarian and maritime security emergency in one of the world’s most critical shipping lanes, with thousands of seafarers caught in the crossfire.  @nbrownIMO— Sea And Coast 🇮🇳 (@seaandcoast1) Apr 24, 2026
properties.author: seaandcoast1

properties.title: Iran May Allow Ships Free Passage Through Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Iran is considering allowing ships to pass freely through the Strait of Hormuz from the Omani side without risk of attack. This potential move could ease tensions and ensure safer passage in a critical global shipping lane. The specifics of this proposal and its implications for international maritime traffic are still developing.

documents.title: There hasn't been a VLCC loaded with Iranian oil transit the Strait of Malacca since April 24  @WindwardAI shows. That's

properties.summary: There hasn't been a VLCC loaded with Iranian oil transit the Strait of Malacca since April 24  @WindwardAI shows. That's a significant and telling figure. Why? It shows that the U.S. blockade has more than halved shipments reaching Asia over the past 12 days, and is the longest streak since the war began. Further declines are now expected into May. This is one of the clearest indicators to date of pressure on Iran’s shipping logistics chain, which supports oil flows worth billions of dollars to the Iranian regime.The chokepoint provides rare visibility into Iranian tanker movements because vessels must broadcast AIS and draft while entering and leaving the busy waterway.For many Iran-linked dark fleet tankers, this is often the first visible signal since loading crude off Kharg Island, Iran’s key export port. Most tankers sail with AIS off before and after this brief window. Not broadcasting a vessel’s position via AIS is a deceptive shipping practice long used by Iran’s dark fleet and contravenes international maritime regulations.AIS signals from Iran-loading dark fleet tankers tracked by Windward show that, for the seven-day period ending April 27, only three VLCCs and one Suezmax entered the Strait of Malacca eastbound. All appear to have loaded before the April 13 embargo was imposed by U.S. forces on vessels sailing to or from Iranian ports.According to Vortexa, none of the seven tankers that were identified loading crude or condensates in Iran since April 13 have completed the 12-day voyage across the Gulf of Oman and Indian Ocean and sailed through the Strait of Malacca.U.S. forces have said they've redirected 39 vessels under the blockade, with dozens of tankers, both laden and in ballast, confined to Iranian territorial waters, off Chabahar, at anchor or drifting in the Gulf of Oman, or manipulating their location via AIS.The pace of Iranian crude exports reaching Asia via the Strait reached 1.3m barrels per day in the first 28 days of April, Windward concludes. That’s lower than 1.9m bpd loaded in March from Kharg Island, & equal to 1.2m bpd seen loading so far in April, according to commodities tracker Vortexa.Vortexa has Iran loading 2.2m bpd in February and some 153m barrels of Iranian oil on the water. Many barrels are in floating storage West of Hormuz or on vessels that sailed to Riau archipelago pre-blockade.This area has long been used for the subterfuge ship-to-ship transfer of Iranian crude to tankers for onward destination to China, the biggest buyer. Most of the Iran tankers that sailed through the Strait of Malacca went to this area, switched off their AIS while transferring the crude cargo to another tanker and then re-emerged when sailing outbound through the Strait. They've been doing this for yearsKeep an eye on the Strait of Malacca, not the Strait of Hormuz -- this is the next pain point. (Image shows Humanity, the last VLCC tracked inbound to the Malacca Strait on April 24)read the Windward daily update here:  windward.ai/blog/april-28-ma…— Michelle Wiese Bockmann (@Michellewb_) Apr 29, 2026

properties.title: There hasn't been a VLCC loaded with Iranian oil transit the Strait of Malacca since April 24  @WindwardAI shows. That's

display.summary: There hasn't been a VLCC loaded with Iranian oil transit the Strait of Malacca since April 24  @WindwardAI shows. That's a significant and telling figure. Why? It shows that the U.S. blockade has more than halved shipments reaching Asia over the past 12 days, and is the longest streak since the war began. Further declines are now expected into May. This is one of the clearest indicators to date of pressure on Iran’s shipping logistics chain, which supports oil flows worth billions of dollars to the Iranian regime.The chokepoint provides rare visibility into Iranian tanker movements because vessels must broadcast AIS and draft while entering and leaving the busy waterway.For many Iran-linked dark fleet tankers, this is often the first visible signal since loading crude off Kharg Island, Iran’s key export port. Most tankers sail with AIS off before and after this brief window. Not broadcasting a vessel’s position via AIS is a deceptive shipping practice long used by Iran’s dark fleet and contravenes international maritime regulations.AIS signals from Iran-loading dark fleet tankers tracked by Windward show that, for the seven-day period ending April 27, only three VLCCs and one Suezmax entered the Strait of Malacca eastbound. All appear to have loaded before the April 13 embargo was imposed by U.S. forces on vessels sailing to or from Iranian ports.According to Vortexa, none of the seven tankers that were identified loading crude or condensates in Iran since April 13 have completed the 12-day voyage across the Gulf of Oman and Indian Ocean and sailed through the Strait of Malacca.U.S. forces have said they've redirected 39 vessels under the blockade, with dozens of tankers, both laden and in ballast, confined to Iranian territorial waters, off Chabahar, at anchor or drifting in the Gulf of Oman, or manipulating their location via AIS.The pace of Iranian crude exports reaching Asia via the Strait reached 1.3m barrels per day in the first 28 days of April, Windward concludes. That’s lower than 1.9m bpd loaded in March from Kharg Island, & equal to 1.2m bpd seen loading so far in April, according to commodities tracker Vortexa.Vortexa has Iran loading 2.2m bpd in February and some 153m barrels of Iranian oil on the water. Many barrels are in floating storage West of Hormuz or on vessels that sailed to Riau archipelago pre-blockade.This area has long been used for the subterfuge ship-to-ship transfer of Iranian crude to tankers for onward destination to China, the biggest buyer. Most of the Iran tankers that sailed through the Strait of Malacca went to this area, switched off their AIS while transferring the crude cargo to another tanker and then re-emerged when sailing outbound through the Strait. They've been doing this for yearsKeep an eye on the Strait of Malacca, not the Strait of Hormuz -- this is the next pain point. (Image shows Humanity, the last VLCC tracked inbound to the Malacca Strait on April 24)read the Windward daily update here:  windward.ai/blog/april-28-ma…— Michelle Wiese Bockmann (@Michellewb_) Apr 29, 2026
