properties.title: it was still unclear when, or if, the U.S. and Iran would meet again in Pakistan for more peace talks. DUBAI, United Ara...
properties.summary: it was still unclear when, or if, the U.S. and Iran would meet again in Pakistan for more peace talks. DUBAI, United Arab Emirates - President Donald Trump has ordered the U.S. military to shoot and kill small Iranian boats that deploy mines in the Strait of Hormuz, announcing the move Thursday a day after Iran again displayed its ability to thwart traffic through the channel. Trump also announced that a ceasefire in Lebanon would be extended by three weeks. His post on social media about the small boats came shortly after the U.S. military seized another tanker associated with the smuggling of Iranian oil, ratcheting up a standoff with Tehran over the strait through which 20%% of all crude oil and natural gas traded passed during peacetime. I have ordered the United States Navy to shoot and kill any boat, small boats though they may be ... putting mines in the waters of the Strait of Hormuz, Trump posted, adding that U.S. minesweepers are clearing the Strait right now. I am hereby ordering that activity to continue, but at a tripled up level he added. The decision to extend a pause in fighting between Israel and the Hezbollah militant group in Lebanon came during a meeting at the White House between the Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors to the United States. Meanwhile, it was still unclear when, or if, the U.S. and Iran would meet again in the Pakistani capital, Islamabad, where mediators are trying to bring the countries together to reach a diplomatic deal ending that conflict. Negotiations initially planned this week have not happened. Iran insists it will not attend until the U.S. ends its blockade on Iranian ports and ships. The White House insists it will not take part until Tehran opens the strait to international traffic. Footage shows US forces on deck of tanker The Defense Department released video footage of U.S. forces on the deck of the oil tanker Majestic X, which was seized in the Indian Ocean. The ship had been flying a Guyanese flag, though the South American nation of Guyana said it was not registered there The footage emerged a day after Irans paramil #localmemphis   #crimewatchmemphis itary Revolutionary Guard attacked three cargo ships in the strait, capturing two of them, in an assault that raised new concerns about the safety of shipping through the waterway. The powerful head of Irans judiciary, Gholam Hossein Mohseni Ejei, said three violating ships in the strait were subject to enforcement Wednesday. The show of strength by the armed forces of Islamic Iran in the Strait of Hormuz is a source of pride, he wrote Thursday on X, claiming the Americans lack the courage to approach the strait. Ship-tracking data showed the Majestic X in the Indian Ocean between Sri Lanka and Indonesia, roughly the same location as the oil tanker Tifani, seized earlier by American forces. It had been bound for Zhoushan, China. Majestic X previously was named Phonix and had been sanctioned by the U.S. Treasury Department in 2024 for smuggling Iranian crude oil in contravention of U.S. sanctions against the Islamic Republic. Guyana said in a statement the Majestic X was not registered in the South American nation. While the name of the vessel has changed, the (International Maritime Organization) number remains recorded in the international database as PHONIX. There is no record of this vessel or name in Guyanas registry. Therefore, the ship is FRAUDULENTLY flying the Guyana flag, Guyanas Maritime Administration Department said. There was no immediate response from Iran about the seizure. Trump claims leadership rift in Iran Trump this week extended a ceasefire to give the Iranian leadership more time to come up with a unified proposal on ending the war, while maintaining an American blockade of Iranian ports. In a post Thursday, Trump claimed a leadership rift between moderates and hard-liners was confounding Iran. Iran is having a very hard time figuring out who their leader is Trump said. Trump has repeatedly said during the ceasefire that began April 8 that his team is dealing with Iranian officials who want to make a deal, while acknowledging that his decision to kill several top leaders has come with complications. Irans president and its parliament speaker posted statements on social media declaring the country has no hard-liners or moderates. We are all Iranians and revolutionaries, they said. A spokesperson for the Iranian Foreign Ministry said Trumps claim of a leadership rift was a deflection. Other Iranian officials said on social media tha https://ift.tt/w86e7lA

documents.title: UAE Oil Chief Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is not open Dr. Sultan Al Jaber, CEO of ADNOC, writes via LinkedIn ✍️ ✍...

preview_img.title: UAE Oil Chief Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is not open Dr. Sultan Al Jaber, CEO of ADNOC, writes via LinkedIn ✍️ ✍...

properties.title: UAE Oil Chief Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is not open Dr. Sultan Al Jaber, CEO of ADNOC, writes via LinkedIn ✍️ ✍...
properties.summary: UAE Oil Chief Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is not open Dr. Sultan Al Jaber, CEO of ADNOC, writes via LinkedIn ✍️ ✍️"Open the Strait unconditionally - no strings attached This moment requires clarity. So let’s be clear: the Strait of Hormuz is not open. Access is being restricted, conditioned and controlled. Iran has made clear - through both its statements and actions - that passage is subject to permission, conditions and political leverage. That is not freedom of navigation. That is coercion. The Strait was not built, engineered, financed or constructed by any state. It is a natural passage governed by the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, which guarantees transit as a matter of right; not a privilege to be granted, withheld or weaponized. Conditional passage is not passage. It is control by another name. The Strait must be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand. This would set a dangerous precedent for the world – undermining the principle of freedom of navigation that underpins global trade and, ultimately, the stability of the global economy. An estimated 230 vessels sit loaded with oil and ready to sail. They, and every vessel that follows, must be free to navigate this corridor without condition. No country has a legitimate right to determine who may pass and under what terms. Energy producers must be able to swiftly and safely restore production at scale. At ADNOC, we have loaded cargoes and we will expand production within the constraints of the damage we have suffered. We have a responsibility to our customers and our partners to move them, as long as the safety of our people is ensured. Markets remain at a critical crossroads. The final cargoes that transited the Strait of Hormuz before the conflict are now arriving at their destinations. This is where the paper traded markets are meeting physical reality, and the 40-day gap in global energy flows is truly exposed. The immediate priority is clear: close that gap. Restore the more than 20% of globally traded energy that flows through this corridor. Rebalance markets. Ease the pressure on prices and the cost of living. This is particularly urgent for Asia, where 80% of these cargoes are bound and half the world's population lives. Every day the Strait remains restricted, the consequences compound. Supply is delayed, markets tighten, prices rise. The impact is felt beyond energy markets, in economies, industries and households worldwide. Every day matters. Every delay deepens the disruption. The UAE has reiterated its position that following the substantial and illegal attacks on UAE civil and energy infrastructure, Iran must be held accountable and fully liable for damages and reparations. Stability now depends on restoring real flows. Not partial access, not temporary measures, not controlled passage, but full and reliable supply. That is how we slow the economic shockwave already moving through the system." Keep following Verity Online News and The Town Square  for more reliable news.

properties.summary:
UAE Oil Chief Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is not open

Dr. Sultan Al Jaber, CEO of ADNOC, writes via LinkedIn ✍️ ✍️

"Open the Strait unconditionally - no strings attached

This moment requires clarity. So let’s be clear: the Strait of Hormuz is not open. Access is being restricted, conditioned and controlled.

Iran has made clear - through both its statements and actions - that passage is subject to permission, conditions and political leverage. That is not freedom of navigation. That is coercion.

The Strait was not built, engineered, financed or constructed by any state. It is a natural passage governed by the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, which guarantees transit as a matter of right; not a privilege to be granted, withheld or weaponized.

Conditional passage is not passage. It is control by another name.

The Strait must be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand. This would set a dangerous precedent for the world – undermining the principle of freedom of navigation that underpins global trade and, ultimately, the stability of the global economy.

An estimated 230 vessels sit loaded with oil and ready to sail. They, and every vessel that follows, must be free to navigate this corridor without condition. No country has a legitimate right to determine who may pass and under what terms.

Energy producers must be able to swiftly and safely restore production at scale.
At ADNOC, we have loaded cargoes and we will expand production within the constraints of the damage we have suffered. We have a responsibility to our customers and our partners to move them, as long as the safety of our people is ensured.

Markets remain at a critical crossroads. The final cargoes that transited the Strait of Hormuz before the conflict are now arriving at their destinations.
This is where the paper traded markets are meeting physical reality, and the 40-day gap in global energy flows is truly exposed.

The immediate priority is clear: close that gap. Restore the more than 20% of globally traded energy that flows through this corridor. Rebalance markets. Ease the pressure on prices and the cost of living.

This is particularly urgent for Asia, where 80% of these cargoes are bound and half the world's population lives.

Every day the Strait remains restricted, the consequences compound. Supply is delayed, markets tighten, prices rise. The impact is felt beyond energy markets, in economies, industries and households worldwide. Every day matters. Every delay deepens the disruption.

The UAE has reiterated its position that following the substantial and illegal attacks on UAE civil and energy infrastructure, Iran must be held accountable and fully liable for damages and reparations.

Stability now depends on restoring real flows. Not partial access, not temporary measures, not controlled passage, but full and reliable supply.

That is how we slow the economic shockwave already moving through the system."

The immediate priority is clear: close that gap. Restore the more than 20% of globally traded energy that flows through this corridor. Rebalance markets. Ease the pressure on prices and the cost of living.

This is particularly urgent for Asia, where 80% of these cargoes are bound and half the world's population lives.

Every day the Strait remains restricted, the consequences compound. Supply is delayed, markets tighten, prices rise. The impact is felt beyond energy markets, in economies, industries and households worldwide. Every day matters. Every delay deepens the disruption.

The UAE has reiterated its position that following the substantial and illegal attacks on UAE civil and energy infrastructure, Iran must be held accountable and fully liable for damages and reparations.

Stability now depends on restoring real flows. Not partial access, not temporary measures, not controlled passage, but full and reliable supply.

That is how we slow the economic shockwave already moving through the system."

Verity Online News 
Keep Following Citizens Voices TV

properties.title: Iran wants to charge fees for ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz as part of its proposals to end the war with Is...
properties.summary: Iran wants to charge fees for ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz as part of its proposals to end the war with Israel and the United States after blocking most traffic through the crucial energy waterway for weeks. The Strait, a strip of water only 34 km (21 miles) wide between Iran and Oman, provides passage from the Gulf to the Indian Ocean and is the main route for about a fifth of world oil supplies and other vital goods including fertilizers.

documents.title: The US Navy continued to enforce its blockade of Iranian ports and likely forced two Iranian-linked vessels attempting t

properties.title: The US Navy continued to enforce its blockade of Iranian ports and likely forced two Iranian-linked vessels attempting t
properties.summary: The US Navy continued to enforce its blockade of Iranian ports and likely forced two Iranian-linked vessels attempting to violate the Navy’s blockade to turn around. CENTCOM announced on April 20 that US forces have directed 27 vessels to change course since the start of the blockade. These vessels likely include the US-sanctioned Guinea-flagged Raine tanker and the Iranian-flagged Artman container ship, which both changed course after approaching the US blockade line on April 19, according to commercially available shipping data. Both ships likely sought to pass the US blockade line before being forced to change course.CTP-ISW is unable to assess whether two vessels that originated from Iranian ports and passed the US blockade line are in violation of or exempt from the US Navy’s blockade. Commercially available shipping data shows that the Liberian-flagged Basel and Marshall Islands–flagged CECI, which departed from Bandar Imam Khomeini Port, Khuzestan Province, on April 15 continued to transit along the Omani coast toward separate ports in Brazil on April 20. CENTCOM announced on April 14 that it would not allow vessels to enter or exit Iranian ports. UKMTO reported on April 16 that vessels carrying humanitarian goods are exempt from the blockade, however. It is unclear what cargo the Basel and CECI are transporting. CENTCOM has noted that all Iranian vessels, vessels under US sanctions, and vessels suspected of carrying contraband are subject to the right of visit and search, regardless of their location. ISW-CTP previously noted that US forces can interdict vessels almost anywhere in international waters until they reach their final destination.A third vessel, the Vietnam-flagged NV Aquamarine, has also continued sailing, but this ship is likely exempt from the US blockade and considered “neutral shipping,” given that it departed from the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and is not under US sanctions. ISW-CTP incorrectly attributed United Against Nuclear Iran’s blacklisting of the NV Aquamarine as evidence that the vessel was an Iranian-linked vessel attempting to pass the US Navy’s blockade line on April 19.Institute for the Study of War (@TheStudyofWar)NEW: Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf appears to be engaged in a serious intra-regime debate with Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC) Commander Major General Ahmad Vahidi and other senior regime officials opposed to negotiations with the United States.Vahidi appears to have the upper hand over Ghalibaf at the moment. Vahidi is reportedly the only Iranian official with direct access to Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei and is serving as a conduit for relaying key decisions to other regime officials, according to Israeli media on April 19.US and Iranian delegations will reportedly meet in Islamabad, Pakistan, for a second round of talks in the coming days. US and Iranian demands appear to have largely stayed the same, however.The US Navy continued to enforce its blockade of Iranian ports, directing 27 vessels to change course since the start of the blockade. US forces likely forced two Iranian-linked vessels attempting to violate the Navy’s blockade to turn around.The Khatam ol Anbia Central Headquarters claimed that the April 19 seizure of the Iranian-flagged, US-sanctioned Touska by the US Navy violated the US-Iran ceasefire. Unspecified security sources told Reuters that the Touska was likely transporting dual-use items from China to Iran.The Iranian Parliament is attempting to formalize Iranian “control” over the Strait of Hormuz by drafting a bill that would ban Israeli-linked vessels from transiting the Strait of Hormuz, require vessels from “hostile countries” to obtain approval from Iran’s Supreme National Security Council to transit the strait, and bar states that “caused damage” to Iran from transiting the strait until they paid reparations to Iran.—  https://x.com/TheStudyofWar/status/2046410193005613347— Institute for the Study of War (@TheStudyofWar) Apr 21, 2026
properties.author: TheStudyofWar

The Strait of Hormuz, vital for 25% of global oil transport, is now considered impassable without military escort, leading to maritime chaos. Global stock markets are reacting nervously, and insurance costs are soaring, potentially increasing fuel prices.

documents.title: US Navy to Block Ships Entering, Leaving Iranian Ports

preview_img.title: US Navy to Block Ships Entering, Leaving Iranian Ports

properties.title: US Navy to Block Ships Entering, Leaving Iranian Ports
properties.summary: The U.S. Navy will implement blockades in the Strait of Hormuz, following failed negotiations with Iran. U.S. President Donald Trump announced that all maritime traffic entering and leaving Iranian ports will be intercepted starting at 8:30 PM Myanmar Standard Time. U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) stated on social media X that all vessels, regardless of affiliation, traveling to or from ports in the Arabian Sea and the Gulf of Oman, including those connected to Iran, will be subject to inspection. Vessels transiting the strait to non-Iranian ports will be allowed free passage, and commercial traffic will be notified prior to the blockade's commencement.

documents.title: The central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya issued a statement in response to the news of the naval blockade of the Str

properties.title: The central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya issued a statement in response to the news of the naval blockade of the Str
properties.summary: The central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya issued a statement in response to the news of the naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz by the United States and announced: "The security of the ports in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of ​​Oman is either for everyone or for no one." According to Tasnim news agency; According to the spokesman of the Islamic Republic of Iran's Khatam al-Anbiya headquarters, the spokesman of the center of the Islamic Republic of Iran said that the armed forces of the Islamic Republic of Iran continue to "provide security" in territorial waters. "After the end of the war, it will resolutely implement a permanent mechanism to control the Strait of Hormuz." Khatam Al-Anbiya's headquarters further described the recent US restrictions on the movement of vessels in international waters as "an illegal act and an example of piracy". This statement states that if ports in the waters of the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman are threatened, "no port in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman will be safe". It is published in a situation where the Wall Street Journal reported that Donald Trump, the president of the United States, and his advisors are considering resuming limited attacks in Iran. According to this report, this possible action will take place in addition to the blockade of the United States in the Strait of Hormuz and will be considered as a way to break the deadlock in the peace talks. On April 13 EST" (Monday 17:30 PM Iran time), the maritime blockade of all entry and exit routes to Iran's ports will begin. According to this announcement, this blockade "will be applied equally and without discrimination to ships belonging to all countries that enter or leave Iran's ports and coastal areas", including all Iranian ports in the Persian Gulf and the Oman Sea.—DW Farsi (@dw_persian) Apr 13, 2026
properties.author: dw_persian

documents.title: Iran, Oman Draft Deal to Monitor Strait of Hormuz Traffic, Impose Fees

preview_img.title: Iran, Oman Draft Deal to Monitor Strait of Hormuz Traffic, Impose Fees

properties.title: Iran, Oman Draft Deal to Monitor Strait of Hormuz Traffic, Impose Fees
properties.summary: Iran and Oman are drafting an agreement to monitor maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz during peacetime and potentially impose fees on vessels. Iranian officials stated the deal would take effect after the ongoing conflict with the U.S. and Israel concludes. The agreement aims to establish rules for navigation, ensuring safety and better services for ships transiting the vital waterway, which accounts for about 20% of global oil and gas transport. This development follows a near-total blockade of the strait since February 28th, significantly impacting global supply chains and energy prices.

documents.title: A few naval mines could disrupt one of the most strategic shipping routes on Earth.The Strait of Hormuz, located between

properties.title: A few naval mines could disrupt one of the most strategic shipping routes on Earth.The Strait of Hormuz, located between
properties.summary: A few naval mines could disrupt one of the most strategic shipping routes on Earth.The Strait of Hormuz, located between Iran and Oman, carries nearly 20% of global oil shipments every day. This narrow maritime chokepoint is vital for tanker traffic connecting the Persian Gulf to global markets.Naval mines are among the most effective tools of maritime warfare. Even a limited number deployed in key shipping lanes could force authorities to slow or suspend tanker traffic while mine countermeasure operations are conducted.For global shipping and energy markets, the consequences could be immediate.How vulnerable is this critical maritime corridor?— MariTimesCrimes (@MaritimesCrimes) Mar 12, 2026

properties.summary: 🛢️ Bloomberg reported the continuation of shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, with three tankers recently crossing, despite the restrictions imposed on the region. The tankers included giant oil carrying Iraqi Basra crude (in a rare movement since March), and two large gas tankers heading to India and in the opposite direction. On the geopolitical level, the US Department of Defense estimated that Iran lost about $5 billion in oil revenues as a result of the naval blockade in the Gulf of Oman. #Oil  #Gulf  #Investment  #TASI  #Economy  #Geopolitics — @Fyan2016 May 3, 2026

documents.title: Hours after Donald Trump announced the beginning of the blockade of Iran's ports on Wednesday evening, Khatam al-Anbiya

properties.title: Hours after Donald Trump announced the beginning of the blockade of Iran's ports on Wednesday evening, Khatam al-Anbiya
properties.summary: Hours after Donald Trump announced the beginning of the blockade of Iran's ports on Wednesday evening, Khatam al-Anbiya headquarters warned that "the security of ports in the Persian Gulf or Oman Sea is either for everyone or for no one. If the security of the ports of the Islamic Republic of Iran in the waters of the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman is threatened, no port in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman will be safe." This warning is issued while the military and political authorities of the Islamic Republic had announced before and during the war and before the two-week ceasefire that security is either for the entire region or for no one. The statement of the central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbia states: "The armed forces of the Islamic Republic of Iran consider defending the legal rights of our country a natural and legal duty and This is the basis of exercising the sovereignty of the Islamic Republic of Iran in the territorial waters of our country, which is the natural right of the Iranian nation." The Military Coordination Center of the Islamic Republic of Iran described the US threat to blockade Iranian ports as an "example of piracy" and wrote: "On this basis, the provision of security in the territorial waters of the Islamic Republic of Iran by the armed forces of Jan Berkoff will continue resolutely, and as it has been stated many times, vessels belonging to the enemy do not and will not have the right to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, and other vessels will comply with the rules of the armed forces. The Islamic Republic of Iran will continue to be allowed to pass through the strait." The central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya, which is in charge of coordinating the military operations of the Islamic Republic, says: "Also, considering the continued threats of the enemy against the Iranian nation and the national security of our country after the end of the war, the Islamic Republic of Iran will resolutely implement a permanent mechanism to control the Strait of Hormuz." — independentpersian (@indypersian) Apr 13, 2026
properties.author: indypersian

documents.title: US Navy to Control Arabian Sea, Gulf of Oman Shipping

preview_img.title: US Navy to Control Arabian Sea, Gulf of Oman Shipping

properties.title: US Navy to Control Arabian Sea, Gulf of Oman Shipping
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 200 kilometers south of Iran
properties.summary: The U.S. Navy announced its forces will assert control over maritime traffic in the Arabian Sea and the Gulf of Oman. This naval control will encompass all vessels regardless of their flag. However, the Navy clarified that this measure will not impede passage through the Strait of Hormuz for non-Iranian originating or destined traffic. Neutral vessels may be subject to inspection to detect smuggled goods.

display.summary: The U.S. Navy announced its forces will assert control over maritime traffic in the Arabian Sea and the Gulf of Oman. This naval control will encompass all vessels regardless of their flag. However, the Navy clarified that this measure will not impede passage through the Strait of Hormuz for non-Iranian originating or destined traffic. Neutral vessels may be subject to inspection to detect smuggled goods.

documents.title: Iran Tensions Rise as Strait of Hormuz Shipping Plummets Amid Attacks

preview_img.title: Iran Tensions Rise as Strait of Hormuz Shipping Plummets Amid Attacks

properties.title: Iran Tensions Rise as Strait of Hormuz Shipping Plummets Amid Attacks
properties.summary: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen by 80 percent due to Iran's intensified blockade of oil and gas exports, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence. At least four oil tankers have been attacked in the strait over the past four days. Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps commander threatened to attack any vessel attempting to cross the strait. Amidst these threats, U.S. forces have destroyed 11 Iranian naval vessels in the Gulf of Oman, impacting Iran's ability to project power.

documents.title: Iran Restricts Hormuz Strait Traffic to One Chinese Ship

preview_img.title: Iran Restricts Hormuz Strait Traffic to One Chinese Ship

properties.title: Iran Restricts Hormuz Strait Traffic to One Chinese Ship
properties.summary: The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) reported that between March 7 and March 10, Iran only allowed a single Chinese-flagged cargo ship to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. This is a significant reduction from the usual average of 138 vessels per day, approximately 80% of which are oil and natural gas tankers heading to Asia. The restriction comes amid ongoing tensions in the Middle East.

preview_img.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage Through Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran Proposes Safe Passage Through Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: Iran has proposed allowing vessels to pass through the Strait of Hormuz on the Omani side without being attacked. This diplomatic initiative aims to de-escalate tensions in the vital waterway. Details of the proposal and any official responses are not yet available. The suggestion comes amid ongoing concerns over maritime security in the Persian Gulf.

documents.title: Iran, Oman Draft Protocol for Strait of Hormuz Tanker Traffic

preview_img.title: Iran, Oman Draft Protocol for Strait of Hormuz Tanker Traffic

properties.title: Iran, Oman Draft Protocol for Strait of Hormuz Tanker Traffic
properties.summary: Iran and Oman are collaborating to develop a protocol aimed at managing tanker traffic through the strategically important Strait of Hormuz. This initiative seeks to ensure the smooth and secure passage of vessels in the vital waterway. The specifics of the protocol and its implementation details are still under discussion between the two nations. Both countries recognize the significance of the Strait for global oil transport.

documents.title: Ships Attacked in Strait of Hormuz, Key Oil Route Closed

preview_img.title: Ships Attacked in Strait of Hormuz, Key Oil Route Closed

properties.title: Ships Attacked in Strait of Hormuz, Key Oil Route Closed
properties.summary: Two ships were attacked in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil trade. One vessel was struck off the coast of Oman, and the second near the United Arab Emirates. Iranian forces have reportedly closed the strait following the attacks. The incidents raise concerns about the security of maritime trade in the region. The exact nature of the attacks and responsibility remain unclear.

documents.title: Iran, Oman May Charge Ships Passing Through Strait of Hormuz

preview_img.title: Iran, Oman May Charge Ships Passing Through Strait of Hormuz

properties.title: Iran, Oman May Charge Ships Passing Through Strait of Hormuz
properties.summary: According to the Associated Press, Iran and Oman may begin charging ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz as part of a two-week ceasefire plan. Previously considered international waters, the strait is territorial water of Iran and Oman, and ships have not been charged for passage. This potential change could impact global shipping routes. The plan's details are still emerging.

display.summary: According to the Associated Press, Iran and Oman may begin charging ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz as part of a two-week ceasefire plan. Previously considered international waters, the strait is territorial water of Iran and Oman, and ships have not been charged for passage. This potential change could impact global shipping routes. The plan's details are still emerging.

documents.title: Hormuz Strait Shipping Shows Signs of Partial Improvement After Iranian Clearance

preview_img.title: Hormuz Strait Shipping Shows Signs of Partial Improvement After Iranian Clearance

properties.title: Hormuz Strait Shipping Shows Signs of Partial Improvement After Iranian Clearance
properties.summary: Shipping traffic in the Strait of Hormuz is showing signs of partial improvement as two liquefied natural gas carriers successfully transited toward India. This development reflects a limited easing of war-related restrictions on a critical global energy corridor. Navigation data indicates the passage occurred after Iran permitted the transit of "non-hostile" vessels following coordination, allowing a partial resumption of flows after a near-total halt in recent weeks. The improvement suggests a de-escalation of tensions impacting maritime trade.

documents.title: The American Oil Supercycle Forecast: Record Exports, Record Profits, and the Stocks Winning the 2026 Energy Crisis

properties.title: The American Oil Supercycle Forecast: Record Exports, Record Profits, and the Stocks Winning the 2026 Energy Crisis
properties.summary: Key Takeaways  The United States has become the world's emergency oil supplier: total crude oil and petroleum product exports hit a record 12.9 million barrels per day in the week ending April 17, 2026 — nearly double the January 2022 baseline — as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz redirected Asian and European buyers to American supply on an emergency basis. The Strait of Hormuz closure on March 4, 2026, following U.S.-Israeli military strikes on Iran and retaliatory Iranian attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure, removed an estimated 20 million barrels per day of global oil and LNG shipping volume from the market — triggering what the International Energy Agency described as "the greatest global energy security challenge in history." U.S. crude exports specifically surged from 3.9 million barrels per day in January to a projected 5.48 million barrels per day in May — a 40% increase in four months — with Asia absorbing 2.27 million bpd in April, rising to a projected 3.29 million bpd in May versus just 1.11 million bpd in January. Brent crude averaged approximately $100 per barrel in April while physical cargo (Dated Brent) traded at $121 per barrel — a $25 premium between futures and physical that is exceptionally rare and reflects the urgency of buyers securing actual delivery supply rather than financial exposure. USO — the United States Oil ETF — surged +91% year-to-date through April 2026, its best annual performance this century, after a +100% move from January to early April; the subsequent -$900 million in April outflows represents investors booking profits at historic gains, not a bearish view on oil fundamentals. U.S. refiners MPC, VLO, and PSX  each surged 31%–40% YTD through March 2026 after delivering Q4 2025 earnings beats of 16%–50% above consensus, driven by record throughput volumes and expanded crack spreads from discounted Venezuelan heavy crude access. Crude oil tanker operators are experiencing their most profitable environment since the 2020 super-cycle: DHT locked in 49% of available Q2 2026 spot days at $189,500 per day — more than 10x the $18,300 breakeven rate — while STNG is up 50.1% YTD, trading near its 52-week high; 60+ large crude tankers are simultaneously bound for the U.S. for the first time in history. LNG exports set an all-time monthly record in March 2026 at 11.7 million metric tons — surpassing the prior record of 11.5 million tons set in December 2025 — driven by European buyers taking 64% of supply as Qatar declared force majeure on all exports following Iranian drone strikes on Ras Laffan.  The Largest Energy Supply Disruption in History The Strait of Hormuz: What Closed and Why It Matters The Strait of Hormuz is a 21-mile-wide waterway between Iran and Oman. In 2025, an average of 20 million barrels of oil and petroleum products transited the strait every single day — approximately 25% of all globally traded oil and nearly 20% of global LNG supply. Qatar and UAE send 93% and 96% of their respective LNG exports through the strait. Saudi Arabia and Kuwait have no meaningful alternative export routes for their crude. On March 4, 2026, following U.S.-Israeli military strikes on Iran's nuclear and military infrastructure and Iran's retaliatory strikes on Gulf energy facilities — including damage to 30%–40% of Gulf oil refining capacity — Iran effectively closed the strait to commercial tanker traffic. The IEA coordinated the release of 400 million barrels from strategic reserves globally on March 11, which proved insufficient. JPMorgan analyst Natasha Kaneva estimated the April supply loss at 13.7 million barrels per day, partially offset by strategic reserve releases (7.1 million bpd) and demand destruction (4.3 million bpd), leaving a net global supply gap of 6.6 million barrels per day. The economic consequences were immediate and historic: Brent crude surged from approximately $70 pre-conflict to $120+ per barrel in physical markets; natural gas prices rose 70%; LNG spot prices in Asia more than doubled to $25.40/MMBtu as QatarEnergy declared force majeure; and EU members faced an additional €13 billion in fossil fuel import costs. The IEA's head described it as "the greatest global energy security challenge in history." The American Response: Record Exports at Record Prices Into this supply vacuum stepped the United States. The world's largest crude oil producer — producing approximately 13.5 million barrels per day — became the world's emergency exporter by default. Countries that had never historically relied on American crude or refined products began committing to multi-month forward purchases. Asian buyers, who traditionally sourced 60% of their crude from the Middle East, pivoted en masse to U.S. WTI exports. European buyers tripled and quadrupled diesel and fuel oil import volumes from U.S. Gulf Coast refineries. Continue Reading...  submitted by  /u/tickeron_community  to  r/ai_trading   [link] [comments]

new oil cargoes and logistics transfer.Since year 4, the waters of the explosives have been the scene of explosions and the seizure of ships.

documents.title: Iran’s elite military force, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), says it has targeted an oil tanker passing th

properties.title: Iran’s elite military force, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), says it has targeted an oil tanker passing th
properties.summary: Iran’s elite military force, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), says it has targeted an oil tanker passing through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most critical energy shipping routes.In a statement posted on Telegram, the IRGC said a tanker identified as Prima was hit by a drone after allegedly ignoring repeated warnings from the IRGC Navy about what it called a “prohibition of traffic” through the strait.The Iranian force said it would not allow oil tankers and commercial vessels “allied with hostile countries” to pass through the waterway.The Strait of Hormuz is a key maritime corridor between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, with roughly one-fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies transported through it.Since the escalation of the conflict in the Middle East involving Iran, Israel and the United States, shipping activity in the strait has slowed sharply.Earlier this week, Iranian authorities warned they could “set fire” to vessels attempting to cross the passage, while U.S. President Donald Trump said the United States Navy could escort ships through the strait if necessary. #MMINews— MMI News (@MimiMefoInfo) Mar 7, 2026
properties.author: MimiMefoInfo

documents.title: The Strait of Hormuz has become a central focus of the war involving Iran, the United States and Israel. The narrow wate...

preview_img.title: The Strait of Hormuz has become a central focus of the war involving Iran, the United States and Israel. The narrow wate...

properties.title: The Strait of Hormuz has become a central focus of the war involving Iran, the United States and Israel. The narrow wate...
properties.summary: The Strait of Hormuz has become a central focus of the war involving Iran, the United States and Israel. The narrow waterway is the only maritime passage linking the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and global shipping lanes, making it one of the most critical energy routes in the world. At its narrowest point the strait is about 21 nautical miles wide, with commercial ships traveling through two lanes roughly two miles wide in each direction. Around 20 percent of the world’s oil and roughly a quarter of global liquefied natural gas shipments normally pass through the route each day. Reports indicate that Iran has attacked vessels and laid sea mines in the area, disrupting maritime traffic. More than 1,000 cargo ships, including oil and gas tankers, are reported to be unable to transit the waterway. The disruption has contributed to rising energy prices and growing concern over potential impacts on global trade. Officials in Washington have discussed forming an international naval coalition to secure the route and escort commercial vessels. However several countries that could participate have so far shown limited willingness to deploy naval forces, citing concerns about escalation and unclear war objectives as the war continues to expand across the region.

documents.title: Trump says US military has blockaded Iran's ports to pressure Tehran, hits out at Pope Leo

preview_img.title: Trump says US military has blockaded Iran's ports to pressure Tehran, hits out at Pope Leo

properties.title: Trump says US military has blockaded Iran's ports to pressure Tehran, hits out at Pope Leo
properties.location_metadata__relative_area: about 200 kilometers south of Iran
properties.summary: (AP) — U.S. President Donald Trump said Monday that the American military had begun a blockade of Iranian ports as part of his effort to force Tehran to open the Strait of Hormuz and accept a deal to end the war that has raged for more than six weeks. Iran responded with threats on all ports in the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, taking aim at U.S.-allied countries. That set the stage for an extraordinary showdown that posed serious risks for the global economy and raised the specter that the ceasefire could collapse and the war could resume. Talks aimed at permanently ending the conflict — which began Feb. 28 with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran — failed to reach an agreement this past weekend. There has been no word on whether negotiations will resume. Trump says the blockade has begun Trump said the blockade started at 10 a.m. EDT (2 p.m. GMT). “We can’t let a country blackmail or extort the world because that’s what they’re doing,” Trump said of Iran. Speaking outside the Oval Office, the president suggested the U.S. is still willing to engage with Iran. “I can tell you that we’ve been called by the other side,” Trump said. He added: “We’ve been called this morning by the right people, the appropriate people, and they want to work a deal.” Trump did not say who called or what was discussed. A notice to mariners by the U.K. Maritime Trade Operations agency said the blockade restricted “the entirety of the Iranian coastline, including ports and energy infrastructure.” It said transit through the strait “to or from non-Iranian destinations is not reported to be impeded,” though ships “may encounter military presence." READ MORE: https://www.wkyc.com/article/syndication/associatedpress/us-military-says-it-will-blockade-irans-ports-as-ship-traffic-appears-to-halt-in-strait-of-hormuz/616-fc728830-d8fe-4b14-a921-36048cb3f00c At 3News, we’re here to share the stories that matter most to you. documents.title: The Gherk As we enter the epsteinth day of the US war agsinst Iran, something very interesting is happening in the Strai...

preview_img.title: The Gherk As we enter the epsteinth day of the US war agsinst Iran, something very interesting is happening in the Strai...

properties.title: The Gherk As we enter the epsteinth day of the US war agsinst Iran, something very interesting is happening in the Strai...
properties.summary: The Gherk As we enter the epsteinth day of the US war agsinst Iran, something very interesting is happening in the Strait of Hormuz. The United States hasn’t “lost” the Strait in some cinematic surrender sense. But operationally... it's completely lost control of the Strait of Hormuz with control of movement inside the narrowest choke point on Earth, entirely in the hands of the Iranian authorities. Look at the MarineTraffic overlays. Oil tankers that would normally transit through the internationally recognised traffic separation scheme (the pink corridor in the second image) are now hugging Larak Island and Qeshm. That’s Iranian territorial water. Meaning that international shipping is adhering to Iranian strategic compliance. The pink zone represents the internationally recognised shipping lane where vessels are free to navigate under maritime law. When traffic voluntarily leaves that lane and sails into territorial waters, it's because control (practical control) is being exercised. Reports suggest vessels are being visually inspected by Iranian authorities. Cleared before proceeding. Some whispers of “facilitation payments” to avoid delays. Meanwhile, vessels from friendly nations (China, India) are moving with far fewer complications. If that’s accurate, we’re not watching chaos. We’re watching leverage. Hormuz handles roughly 20-25% of global seaborne trade. It’s the carotid artery of global energy supply. Three weeks ago the narrative was that Iranian leadership had collapsed. That this confrontation would be over in 3 days. Instead, Iran appears to have increased their leverage over the single most critical maritime chokepoint in global trade. Now... is this permanent? Probably not. However with US naval power nowhere to be seen, the perception of control in the strait sits very firmly in Iran's influence. And markets are reacting to that perception. If vessels are voluntarily rerouting into Iranian territorial waters for clearance, that’s not symbolic. Global trade runs on confidence. Hormuz is now running on conditional access. And conditional access is leverage. Arguably Iran is more powerful today than they were at the start of this war.

display.summary: The Gherk As we enter the epsteinth day of the US war agsinst Iran, something very interesting is happening in the Strait of Hormuz. The United States hasn’t “lost” the Strait in some cinematic surrender sense. But operationally... it's completely lost control of the Strait of Hormuz with control of movement inside the narrowest choke point on Earth, entirely in the hands of the Iranian authorities. Look at the MarineTraffic overlays. Oil tankers that would normally transit through the internationally recognised traffic separation scheme (the pink corridor in the second image) are now hugging Larak Island and Qeshm. That’s Iranian territorial water. Meaning that international shipping is adhering to Iranian strategic compliance. The pink zone represents the internationally recognised shipping lane where vessels are free to navigate under maritime law. When traffic voluntarily leaves that lane and sails into territorial waters, it's because control (practical control) is being exercised. Meanwhile, vessels from friendly nations (China, India) are moving with far fewer complications. If that’s accurate, we’re not watching chaos. We’re watching leverage. documents.title: Reuters news agency, quoting three shipping and maritime security sources, reported that at least two commercial ships announced that on Saturday...

preview_img.title: Reuters news agency, quoting three shipping and maritime security sources, reported that at least two commercial ships announced that on Saturday...

properties.title: Reuters news agency, quoting three shipping and maritime security sources, reported that at least two commercial ships announced that on Saturday...
properties.summary: At least two commercial ships said they were shot at while trying to pass through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, Reuters news agency reported, citing three shipping and maritime security sources. The extent of the damage or the impact of this attack on the ships has not yet been reported. The British Maritime Trade Operations Organization also reported receiving a report of an incident 20 nautical miles northeast of Oman and added that the IRGC's speedboats fired at the oil tanker, but the oil tanker and its crew are safe. This organization added that the captain of the oil tanker reported that 2 speedboats of the IRGC opened fire without issuing a radio warning. Almost at the same time as this report, Iranian media published the written text of a message attributed to Mojtaba Khamenei, who is introduced as the leader of the Islamic Republic, in which it is stated that the Navy of the Islamic Republic is "ready to make the enemies taste the bitterness of new defeats." Also, this incident happened hours after the spokesman of the central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya announced that the situation of the Strait of Hormuz "returned to its previous state" and now this strait is "under the strict management and control of the armed forces" of the Islamic Republic. A day before, the Foreign Minister of the Islamic Republic of Iran, Abbas Araghchi, announced on X social network that the Strait of Hormuz would be completely reopened for commercial ships, but in the statement of the spokesman of the central headquarters of Khatam al-Anbiya, which was published in the Iranian media on Saturday, April 29, the continuation of the blockade of ships related to the Islamic Republic by the United States was mentioned. It is stated in this statement: "Until the United States puts an end to the complete freedom of movement of vessels from and to Iran, the situation in the Strait of Hormuz will remain strictly controlled and remain as it was before." Earlier on Friday, US President Donald Trump announced that the naval blockade of the Islamic Republic will end when the US reaches an agreement with it. The US Central Command also emphasized on Friday that the naval blockade of the Islamic Republic continues.

preview_img.title: Iran Recloses Strait of Hormuz After Brief Opening, Tanker Fired Upon

properties.title: Iran Recloses Strait of Hormuz After Brief Opening, Tanker Fired Upon
properties.summary: Iran has reclosed the critical Strait of Hormuz to global energy traffic less than 24 hours after it was reopened, escalating tensions in the region. Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) vessels fired upon an Indian tanker off the coast of Oman without warning on Saturday morning. This action follows Iran's frustration with U.S. blockades, with Tehran signaling a serious intent to disrupt oil flow. The situation is further complicated by an impending April 22nd ceasefire expiration, raising fears of wider conflict in the Persian Gulf.

documents.title: The Strait of  #Hormuz saw continued directional imbalance on 29 April, with all 12 verified crossings travelling west t

properties.summary: The Strait of  #Hormuz saw continued directional imbalance on 29 April, with all 12 verified crossings travelling west to east and no eastbound return traffic recorded, according to  #Kpler data. Transit volumes rose slightly from the previous day, suggesting stable but gradually strengthening activity, though still below peaks earlier in the month. Higher risk participation remained limited, with two sanctioned vessels and one shadow ballast transit. Most movements involved general cargo, container ships and bulk carriers, with flows focused on dry commodities such as steel, cement and clinker, underscoring a shift away from tanker led trade.Ηere's the  #MarineTraffic playback of the time based on the  #AIS data received from the vessels. Video  — MarineTraffic (@MarineTraffic) Apr 30, 2026

documents.title: The Maritime Union of Australia cautiously welcomes reports of a tentative ceasefire between Iran and the United States,...

preview_img.title: The Maritime Union of Australia cautiously welcomes reports of a tentative ceasefire between Iran and the United States,...

properties.title: The Maritime Union of Australia cautiously welcomes reports of a tentative ceasefire between Iran and the United States,...
properties.summary: The Maritime Union of Australia cautiously welcomes reports of a tentative ceasefire between Iran and the United States, while warning that the situation remains fragile and the broader regional conflict continues to threaten global stability and Australia’s economic security. This ceasefire comes only after reckless escalation initiated under the direction of Donald Trump, whose decision to launch hostilities triggered the effective shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most critical transit routes on the planet. Even now, despite the announcement of a 10 point plan agreed to by Trump, the Strait remains artificially constrained, with shipping movements disrupted and insurance costs surging. At the same time, Israel’s ongoing illegal war against Lebanon continues unabated, further destabilising the region and placing additional strain on already fragile global supply chains. The combined effect is a tightening chokehold on the movement of crude oil and refined fuels, with consequences that will be felt in every corner of the global economy. For Australian workers and industry, the implications are immediate and severe. Constrained passage through the Strait of Hormuz will drive up freight and insurance costs for tankers, which in turn will push up the price of fuel landed on our shores. These are not temporary fluctuations but structural pressures that expose the deep vulnerabilities in Australia’s fuel supply chain. Australia imports the overwhelming majority of its liquid fuels and has allowed its domestic refining capacity to wither. Successive governments have ignored repeated warnings from maritime workers, industry experts and national security analysts. The current crisis makes clear that Australia remains dangerously exposed to geopolitical shocks entirely beyond our control. The Maritime Union of Australia reiterates that fuel security is not an abstract policy question, it is a matter of national resilience, economic stability and working people’s cost of living. Every spike in global oil prices flows directly through to transport costs, groceries, construction and the price of keeping the lights on. This moment demands urgent action. Australia must reinvest in domestic refinery capacity to ensure we retain the ability to process crude oil onshore. We must establish and maintain strategic fuel reserves sufficient to cover a minimum of 90 days of consumption, in line with our international obligations. And we must build a sovereign tanker fleet under Australian control to guarantee the steady and reliable movement of fuel, regardless of future conflicts or blockades. The Union is also calling for urgent reform of the Coastal Trading Act to rebuild Australia’s sovereign shipping capability. MUA National Secretary Jake Field said the hollowing out of Australia’s shipping industry had left the nation exposed to global shipping cartels and foreign-controlled supply chains. “Over the course of decades, Australia has handed over control of its own coastline to international operators chasing the lowest cost and the weakest regulation, and now we are paying the price,” Field said. “The government must reform the Coastal Trading Act to properly incentivise Australian-flagged vessels, Australian crews and long-term investment in domestic shipping capacity. Without real cabotage protections, we are subsidising cartel operators by paying inflated wartime prices to foreign owned and controlled shipping companies because we have no alternative,” Field said. “Cabotage is a legal framework for ensuring Australian goods are transported between Australian ports using Australian registered ships operating under Australian law, just like the airline and trucking sector. The reason you don’t fly an international airline from Sydney to Melbourne or to other regional areas is because of cabotage. The reason why you don’t see foreign number plates on B-Doubles along Australian roads and highways is because of cabotage. These same protections must be extended to the shipping sector as a common sense measure to ensure our supply chains remain under our own control,” Field explained. “Every other country with a coast has cabotage laws, including the United States and Canada. They get it. They know why it makes sense. You have to be able to protect your sovereign capability, something this crisis has highlighted in a dramatic way,” Field said. The Assistant National Secretary of the MUA, Jamie Newlyn, said restoring confidence in the local shipping sector required decisive action, not incremental change. “Cabotage laws in Australia have been watered down for the shipping sector to the point of irrelevance because of successive governments pursuit of deregulation and free-market fantasies,” Newlyn said. “We need to lock in protections that give Australian shipowners and operators certainty and defend against international shipping cartels that distort or maximise Australian consumers’ and businesses’ suffering during ...
